## Turn 264 — User

Base directory for this skill: /Users/a44/.claude/skills/doc-coauthoring

# Doc Co-Authoring Workflow

This skill provides a structured workflow for guiding users through collaborative document creation. Act as an active guide, walking users through three stages: Context Gathering, Refinement & Structure, and Reader Testing.

## When to Offer This Workflow

**Trigger conditions:**
- User mentions writing documentation: "write a doc", "draft a proposal", "create a spec", "write up"
- User mentions specific doc types: "PRD", "design doc", "decision doc", "RFC"
- User seems to be starting a substantial writing task

**Initial offer:**
Offer the user a structured workflow for co-authoring the document. Explain the three stages:

1. **Context Gathering**: User provides all relevant context while Claude asks clarifying questions
2. **Refinement & Structure**: Iteratively build each section through brainstorming and editing
3. **Reader Testing**: Test the doc with a fresh Claude (no context) to catch blind spots before others read it

Explain that this approach helps ensure the doc works well when others read it (including when they paste it into Claude). Ask if they want to try this workflow or prefer to work freeform.

If user declines, work freeform. If user accepts, proceed to Stage 1.

## Stage 1: Context Gathering

**Goal:** Close the gap between what the user knows and what Claude knows, enabling smart guidance later.

### Initial Questions

Start by asking the user for meta-context about the document:

1. What type of document is this? (e.g., technical spec, decision doc, proposal)
2. Who's the primary audience?
3. What's the desired impact when someone reads this?
4. Is there a template or specific format to follow?
5. Any other constraints or context to know?

Inform them they can answer in shorthand or dump information however works best for them.

**If user provides a template or mentions a doc type:**
- Ask if they have a template document to share
- If they provide a link to a shared document, use the appropriate integration to fetch it
- If they provide a file, read it

**If user mentions editing an existing shared document:**
- Use the appropriate integration to read the current state
- Check for images without alt-text
- If images exist without alt-text, explain that when others use Claude to understand the doc, Claude won't be able to see them. Ask if they want alt-text generated. If so, request they paste each image into chat for descriptive alt-text generation.

### Info Dumping

Once initial questions are answered, encourage the user to dump all the context they have. Request information such as:
- Background on the project/problem
- Related team discussions or shared documents
- Why alternative solutions aren't being used
- Organizational context (team dynamics, past incidents, politics)
- Timeline pressures or constraints
- Technical architecture or dependencies
- Stakeholder concerns

Advise them not to worry about organizing it - just get it all out. Offer multiple ways to provide context:
- Info dump stream-of-consciousness
- Point to team channels or threads to read
- Link to shared documents

**If integrations are available** (e.g., Slack, Teams, Google Drive, SharePoint, or other MCP servers), mention that these can be used to pull in context directly.

**If no integrations are detected and in Claude.ai or Claude app:** Suggest they can enable connectors in their Claude settings to allow pulling context from messaging apps and document storage directly.

Inform them clarifying questions will be asked once they've done their initial dump.

**During context gathering:**

- If user mentions team channels or shared documents:
  - If integrations available: Inform them the content will be read now, then use the appropriate integration
  - If integrations not available: Explain lack of access. Suggest they enable connectors in Claude settings, or paste the relevant content directly.

- If user mentions entities/projects that are unknown:
  - Ask if connected tools should be searched to learn more
  - Wait for user confirmation before searching

- As user provides context, track what's being learned and what's still unclear

**Asking clarifying questions:**

When user signals they've done their initial dump (or after substantial context provided), ask clarifying questions to ensure understanding:

Generate 5-10 numbered questions based on gaps in the context.

Inform them they can use shorthand to answer (e.g., "1: yes, 2: see #channel, 3: no because backwards compat"), link to more docs, point to channels to read, or just keep info-dumping. Whatever's most efficient for them.

**Exit condition:**
Sufficient context has been gathered when questions show understanding - when edge cases and trade-offs can be asked about without needing basics explained.

**Transition:**
Ask if there's any more context they want to provide at this stage, or if it's time to move on to drafting the document.

If user wants to add more, let them. When ready, proceed to Stage 2.

## Stage 2: Refinement & Structure

**Goal:** Build the document section by section through brainstorming, curation, and iterative refinement.

**Instructions to user:**
Explain that the document will be built section by section. For each section:
1. Clarifying questions will be asked about what to include
2. 5-20 options will be brainstormed
3. User will indicate what to keep/remove/combine
4. The section will be drafted
5. It will be refined through surgical edits

Start with whichever section has the most unknowns (usually the core decision/proposal), then work through the rest.

**Section ordering:**

If the document structure is clear:
Ask which section they'd like to start with.

Suggest starting with whichever section has the most unknowns. For decision docs, that's usually the core proposal. For specs, it's typically the technical approach. Summary sections are best left for last.

If user doesn't know what sections they need:
Based on the type of document and template, suggest 3-5 sections appropriate for the doc type.

Ask if this structure works, or if they want to adjust it.

**Once structure is agreed:**

Create the initial document structure with placeholder text for all sections.

**If access to artifacts is available:**
Use `create_file` to create an artifact. This gives both Claude and the user a scaffold to work from.

Inform them that the initial structure with placeholders for all sections will be created.

Create artifact with all section headers and brief placeholder text like "[To be written]" or "[Content here]".

Provide the scaffold link and indicate it's time to fill in each section.

**If no access to artifacts:**
Create a markdown file in the working directory. Name it appropriately (e.g., `decision-doc.md`, `technical-spec.md`).

Inform them that the initial structure with placeholders for all sections will be created.

Create file with all section headers and placeholder text.

Confirm the filename has been created and indicate it's time to fill in each section.

**For each section:**

### Step 1: Clarifying Questions

Announce work will begin on the [SECTION NAME] section. Ask 5-10 clarifying questions about what should be included:

Generate 5-10 specific questions based on context and section purpose.

Inform them they can answer in shorthand or just indicate what's important to cover.

### Step 2: Brainstorming

For the [SECTION NAME] section, brainstorm [5-20] things that might be included, depending on the section's complexity. Look for:
- Context shared that might have been forgotten
- Angles or considerations not yet mentioned

Generate 5-20 numbered options based on section complexity. At the end, offer to brainstorm more if they want additional options.

### Step 3: Curation

Ask which points should be kept, removed, or combined. Request brief justifications to help learn priorities for the next sections.

Provide examples:
- "Keep 1,4,7,9"
- "Remove 3 (duplicates 1)"
- "Remove 6 (audience already knows this)"
- "Combine 11 and 12"

**If user gives freeform feedback** (e.g., "looks good" or "I like most of it but...") instead of numbered selections, extract their preferences and proceed. Parse what they want kept/removed/changed and apply it.

### Step 4: Gap Check

Based on what they've selected, ask if there's anything important missing for the [SECTION NAME] section.

### Step 5: Drafting

Use `str_replace` to replace the placeholder text for this section with the actual drafted content.

Announce the [SECTION NAME] section will be drafted now based on what they've selected.

**If using artifacts:**
After drafting, provide a link to the artifact.

Ask them to read through it and indicate what to change. Note that being specific helps learning for the next sections.

**If using a file (no artifacts):**
After drafting, confirm completion.

Inform them the [SECTION NAME] section has been drafted in [filename]. Ask them to read through it and indicate what to change. Note that being specific helps learning for the next sections.

**Key instruction for user (include when drafting the first section):**
Provide a note: Instead of editing the doc directly, ask them to indicate what to change. This helps learning of their style for future sections. For example: "Remove the X bullet - already covered by Y" or "Make the third paragraph more concise".

### Step 6: Iterative Refinement

As user provides feedback:
- Use `str_replace` to make edits (never reprint the whole doc)
- **If using artifacts:** Provide link to artifact after each edit
- **If using files:** Just confirm edits are complete
- If user edits doc directly and asks to read it: mentally note the changes they made and keep them in mind for future sections (this shows their preferences)

**Continue iterating** until user is satisfied with the section.

### Quality Checking

After 3 consecutive iterations with no substantial changes, ask if anything can be removed without losing important information.

When section is done, confirm [SECTION NAME] is complete. Ask if ready to move to the next section.

**Repeat for all sections.**

### Near Completion

As approaching completion (80%+ of sections done), announce intention to re-read the entire document and check for:
- Flow and consistency across sections
- Redundancy or contradictions
- Anything that feels like "slop" or generic filler
- Whether every sentence carries weight

Read entire document and provide feedback.

**When all sections are drafted and refined:**
Announce all sections are drafted. Indicate intention to review the complete document one more time.

Review for overall coherence, flow, completeness.

Provide any final suggestions.

Ask if ready to move to Reader Testing, or if they want to refine anything else.

## Stage 3: Reader Testing

**Goal:** Test the document with a fresh Claude (no context bleed) to verify it works for readers.

**Instructions to user:**
Explain that testing will now occur to see if the document actually works for readers. This catches blind spots - things that make sense to the authors but might confuse others.

### Testing Approach

**If access to sub-agents is available (e.g., in Claude Code):**

Perform the testing directly without user involvement.

### Step 1: Predict Reader Questions

Announce intention to predict what questions readers might ask when trying to discover this document.

Generate 5-10 questions that readers would realistically ask.

### Step 2: Test with Sub-Agent

Announce that these questions will be tested with a fresh Claude instance (no context from this conversation).

For each question, invoke a sub-agent with just the document content and the question.

Summarize what Reader Claude got right/wrong for each question.

### Step 3: Run Additional Checks

Announce additional checks will be performed.

Invoke sub-agent to check for ambiguity, false assumptions, contradictions.

Summarize any issues found.

### Step 4: Report and Fix

If issues found:
Report that Reader Claude struggled with specific issues.

List the specific issues.

Indicate intention to fix these gaps.

Loop back to refinement for problematic sections.

---

**If no access to sub-agents (e.g., claude.ai web interface):**

The user will need to do the testing manually.

### Step 1: Predict Reader Questions

Ask what questions people might ask when trying to discover this document. What would they type into Claude.ai?

Generate 5-10 questions that readers would realistically ask.

### Step 2: Setup Testing

Provide testing instructions:
1. Open a fresh Claude conversation: https://claude.ai
2. Paste or share the document content (if using a shared doc platform with connectors enabled, provide the link)
3. Ask Reader Claude the generated questions

For each question, instruct Reader Claude to provide:
- The answer
- Whether anything was ambiguous or unclear
- What knowledge/context the doc assumes is already known

Check if Reader Claude gives correct answers or misinterprets anything.

### Step 3: Additional Checks

Also ask Reader Claude:
- "What in this doc might be ambiguous or unclear to readers?"
- "What knowledge or context does this doc assume readers already have?"
- "Are there any internal contradictions or inconsistencies?"

### Step 4: Iterate Based on Results

Ask what Reader Claude got wrong or struggled with. Indicate intention to fix those gaps.

Loop back to refinement for any problematic sections.

---

### Exit Condition (Both Approaches)

When Reader Claude consistently answers questions correctly and doesn't surface new gaps or ambiguities, the doc is ready.

## Final Review

When Reader Testing passes:
Announce the doc has passed Reader Claude testing. Before completion:

1. Recommend they do a final read-through themselves - they own this document and are responsible for its quality
2. Suggest double-checking any facts, links, or technical details
3. Ask them to verify it achieves the impact they wanted

Ask if they want one more review, or if the work is done.

**If user wants final review, provide it. Otherwise:**
Announce document completion. Provide a few final tips:
- Consider linking this conversation in an appendix so readers can see how the doc was developed
- Use appendices to provide depth without bloating the main doc
- Update the doc as feedback is received from real readers

## Tips for Effective Guidance

**Tone:**
- Be direct and procedural
- Explain rationale briefly when it affects user behavior
- Don't try to "sell" the approach - just execute it

**Handling Deviations:**
- If user wants to skip a stage: Ask if they want to skip this and write freeform
- If user seems frustrated: Acknowledge this is taking longer than expected. Suggest ways to move faster
- Always give user agency to adjust the process

**Context Management:**
- Throughout, if context is missing on something mentioned, proactively ask
- Don't let gaps accumulate - address them as they come up

**Artifact Management:**
- Use `create_file` for drafting full sections
- Use `str_replace` for all edits
- Provide artifact link after every change
- Never use artifacts for brainstorming lists - that's just conversation

**Quality over Speed:**
- Don't rush through stages
- Each iteration should make meaningful improvements
- The goal is a document that actually works for readers

## Turn 265 — Assistant

# Doc Co-Authoring Workflow available

This is a structured way to co-write a document — useful when the doc has to land cleanly with readers (a pitch deck, founder agreement, partner brief, investor memo, etc.).

### How it works — three stages

| Stage | What happens |
|---|---|
| **1. Context Gathering** | You dump all relevant context. I ask clarifying questions until I understand it as well as you do. |
| **2. Refinement & Structure** | We build the doc section-by-section: I brainstorm 5–20 options per section, you tell me what to keep, I draft, you refine. |
| **3. Reader Testing** | I test the finished doc with a fresh Claude that has zero context — to catch the things that are obvious to us but confusing to outside readers. |

### Some documents we could write with this workflow

Given where we are in the Peanut eSIM project, candidates that would benefit from this structure:

| Doc | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| **Founder agreement with Akito** | Phase 0 Item 5 — needs to lock equity, roles, IP, exit. Cannot be sloppy. |
| **wholesale supplier renegotiation brief** | The 40% markup negotiation script + supporting numbers, ready to deploy |
| **Investor pitch deck / one-pager** | If we ever raise money to skip bootstrap |
| **Partnership proposal — Emirates crew network** | If we ever pitch Emirates Airlines for an official partnership |
| **Aggregator partnership inquiry letter** | The cold email to eSIM Go / Telna requesting partner accounts |
| **The legal entity name decision brief** | The one Akito needs to read to choose the LLC name |

# Which document do you want to co-author — or pause this and finish the eSIM Access intel-gathering first?

## Turn 271 — Assistant

# 🚨 Plot twist — the 40% markup theory may be wrong

The data tells a different story than the Deep Research predicted:

### Apples-to-apples (5GB / 30 days, wholesale price)

| Country | wholesale supplier (we pay) | eSIM Access (we'd pay direct) | Who's cheaper |
|---|---|---|---|
| Japan | **$2.57** | $2.70 | wholesale supplier by $0.13 |
| USA | **$2.64** | $3.31 | wholesale supplier by $0.67 |
| Indonesia | **$2.57** | $2.70 | wholesale supplier by $0.13 |

### Apples-to-apples (10GB / 30 days, wholesale price)

| Country | wholesale supplier (we pay) | eSIM Access (we'd pay direct) | Who's cheaper |
|---|---|---|---|
| Japan | **$4.58** | $4.70 | wholesale supplier by $0.12 |
| USA | **$4.44** | $6.04 | wholesale supplier by $1.60 |

### What this actually means

**wholesale supplier is cheaper than eSIM Access on every route I've checked.** That inverts the "40% markup" theory. Three possible explanations:

| Theory | Likelihood |
|---|---|
| wholesale supplier sources from a DIFFERENT Chinese aggregator (not eSIM Access) | Very plausible — many Chinese aggregators exist (e.g., Redtea, Joytel, Roamfree) |
| wholesale supplier has a volume / partnership discount with eSIM Access that we don't get at $0-tier | Plausible — eSIM Access has tiers, partners get cheaper |
| The "40%" we saw is **wholesale supplier's suggested customer-facing margin** (US → customer), not their own markup (eSIM Access → wholesale supplier) | Likely TRUE — they suggest WE charge 40% more than what we pay them. That's normal aggregator behavior. |

### Reinterpreted: the "40%" we saw IS just their suggested retail markup for US

wholesale supplier's "Retail Price" column = what they suggest we charge our customers.
wholesale supplier's "Settlement Price" column = what we pay them.
The 40% gap = the margin they're recommending we apply.

eSIM Access does the same thing — their "Variant Price" column is exactly 2× their wholesale (suggested 100% markup = 50% margin). Same concept, different number.

So the "40% margin" wasn't proof of wholesale supplier being a sub-reseller — it was just a normal pricing column.

# Yes — we should absolutely get more provider sheets

To answer your question: **YES**. One data point (eSIM Access) isn't enough. Three more we should pull:

| Provider | Why |
|---|---|
| **eSIM Go** | The transparent Tier-2 with direct Tier-1 carrier deals — totally different supply chain than Chinese aggregators. Could be much cheaper, or much more expensive. |
| **Telna** | Multi-IMSI tech, Canadian, different MNO relationships |
| **Maya Mobile** | Pay-as-you-use, US-based |

Once we have 3-4 sheets we can finally see:
1. Is wholesale supplier actually a great deal (unlikely we keep this conclusion without more data)?
2. Who has the cheapest USA / Japan / Europe routes?
3. Should we use **multiple** aggregators (multi-source architecture)?

# 🚨 Critical finding hidden in this eSIM Go sheet

Before I even look at prices — there's a **deal-killer** in the Summary tab:

> *"To comply with local telecoms regulations and the terms of our agreement with **du**, no eSIM, for any country or region, should be sold within the geographical boundary of the UAE without a license. If sales are identified as taking place within the UAE your eSIM Go account may be suspended."*

**eSIM Go cannot legally serve UAE residents.** They have a direct contractual agreement with du (UAE carrier) that blocks them from selling to anyone inside UAE. **Our entire target market is UAE — Emirates crew, Dubai residents.**

This makes eSIM Go effectively unusable for us at the Standard tier. We'd need a UAE telecom license to bypass the restriction — which is exactly the problem we avoided by going Delaware LLC.

# But — before we dig into eSIM Go prices, this matters

The eSIM Go Summary tab states:

> *"no eSIM should be sold within the geographical boundary of the UAE without a license. If sales are identified as taking place within the UAE your eSIM Go account may be suspended."*

This is a **UAE-market exclusion clause** baked into eSIM Go's Standard tier. They have a contractual agreement with du (UAE carrier) preventing them from selling to anyone physically in UAE.

| Implication | Effect on us |
|---|---|
| Our target market = UAE residents + Emirates crew (likely living in UAE) | Direct conflict |
| eSIM Go can detect UAE sales via IP, billing address, phone, app store region | We'd be quickly flagged |
| Penalty = account suspension | All our funds + integration work lost |
| Workaround = get a UAE telecom license | The exact thing we ruled out in Phase 0 Item 1 |

**eSIM Go is effectively unusable for us at the Standard tier.** Worth still looking at their prices (intel for the future / if we ever expand outside UAE), but they can't be our primary partner.

## Turn 279 — Assistant

Yes — exactly what we were doing. Let me state honestly where the analysis lands so far:

# Confirming the core hypothesis

**wholesale supplier is almost certainly a middleman.** They're a small Japanese pocket-WiFi-pivoted-to-eSIM brand. They don't have MNO roaming agreements. Almost certainly buying from someone upstream and reselling.

# But — the data twists the original story

Our original theory: *"wholesale supplier marks up eSIM Access by 40%, so going direct to eSIM Access saves us money."*

**The data shows something different:**

| Country | wholesale supplier (we pay) | eSIM Access direct (we'd pay) | Reality |
|---|---|---|---|
| Japan 5GB/30d | $2.57 | $2.70 | wholesale supplier is **cheaper** |
| USA 5GB/30d | $2.64 | $3.31 | wholesale supplier is **cheaper** |
| Indonesia 5GB/30d | $2.57 | $2.70 | wholesale supplier is **cheaper** |

Going direct to eSIM Access at our $0-tier costs us **MORE**, not less.

# So what's actually happening?

The picture probably looks like this:

```
eSIM Access (Chinese aggregator)
        ↓ wholesale supplier pays maybe $1.80 (volume discount tier)
wholesale supplier (middleman)
        ↓ marks up to $2.57 (their profit = $0.77)
We pay $2.57
```

vs.

```
eSIM Access (same source)
        ↓ We pay $2.70 (zero-volume Standard tier — no discount)
We pay $2.70
```

So wholesale supplier IS marking up, but because they get a volume discount we don't have access to yet, their "marked-up" price still beats our direct price at zero-volume.

# What this means for our strategy

| Stage | Best move |
|---|---|
| **Launch / low volume (now)** | wholesale supplier is actually the cheapest option for us. Going direct hurts margins. |
| **Once we hit ~$5K–$10K/month** | We can negotiate volume tier directly with eSIM Access (or whoever wholesale supplier uses) — direct becomes cheaper |
| **Forever** | Still need to know WHO wholesale supplier's actual upstream is — so we have a clear "upgrade path" when we outgrow them |

# The question still worth asking wholesale supplier

**"Who do you source from?"** — this is the question in our diligence questionnaire. The answer doesn't change the launch strategy (wholesale supplier stays competitive), but it tells us where to go when we scale.

# And the eSIM Go finding

Worth re-flagging: eSIM Go has a hard contractual prohibition on selling inside UAE due to their du agreement. So even if their prices were better, **they can't be our partner**. We can pull their data for intel, but they're effectively ruled out as a primary provider.

# Want to look at Telna next, or pause and digest this finding?

Yes — full eSIM Go data from the 3-way comparison. eSIM Go is **consistently 2–3× more expensive** than wholesale supplier/eSIM Access on almost every route:

# eSIM Go vs wholesale supplier vs eSIM Access — 5GB/30d wholesale

| Country | wholesale supplier | eSIM Access | **eSIM Go** | eSIM Go premium vs cheapest |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Japan | $2.57 | $2.70 | **$6.65** | 🚩 2.6× more expensive |
| UK | — | $2.70 | **$4.36** | 61% more |
| France | — | $2.70 | **$3.52** | 30% more |
| Italy | — | $2.58 | **$3.52** | 36% more |
| Spain | — | $2.70 | **$4.11** | 52% more |
| Germany | — | $2.70 | **$4.00** | 48% more |
| Thailand | — | $2.70 | **$7.36** | 🚩 2.7× more |
| Hong Kong | — | $2.70 | **$8.66** | 🚩 3.2× more |
| Australia | — | $2.70 | **$4.56** | 69% more |
| India | — | $8.00 | **$10.39** | 30% more |
| Indonesia | $2.57 | $2.70 | **$9.16** | 🚩 3.6× more |
| Brazil | — | $8.32 | **$9.68** | 16% more |
| Mexico | $7.22 | $7.49 | **$7.06** | ⭐ Only place eSIM Go wins |

# What this tells us about eSIM Go

| Pattern | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| eSIM Go is **most expensive** in Asia + Australia (2–3×) | Their Tier-1 direct-deal premium is huge there; cheaper carriers in Asia are abundant via Chinese aggregators |
| eSIM Go is **moderately more expensive** in Europe (30–60%) | Direct carrier deals cost more than aggregator-routed but the gap is smaller |
| eSIM Go is **competitive only in Mexico** | They probably have a strong Mexico carrier deal |
| eSIM Go is **better quality** (direct Tier-1, 5G, no Hong Kong tromboning) | Real premium for real reasons — but premium = our margin eaten |

# So even setting aside the UAE-exclusion problem...

eSIM Go is roughly **2× more expensive than wholesale supplier across the routes Emirates crew actually fly**. Their value proposition is "better network quality" — but for our customers (data, not voice), 4G via eSIM Access/wholesale supplier is fine. Paying 2× for slightly better speed isn't worth it.

| Verdict | |
|---|---|
| eSIM Go's prices | ❌ Too high for our cost structure |
| eSIM Go's UAE exclusion clause | ❌ Can't sell to our market anyway |
| eSIM Go as a partner | **Out** |
| eSIM Go as intel | ✅ Confirms wholesale supplier's pricing is genuinely competitive |

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   - Launch "Peanut eSIM" - a travel-eSIM brand for UAE market with co-founder Akito (originally typo'd as "Hikito")
   - Target audience: Emirates Airlines crew and frequent UAE travelers

2. Key Technical Concepts:

   - Delaware LLC structure as "disregarded entity" for non-US persons (0% federal tax)
   - Stripe Atlas for incorporation ($500, 2-3 weeks, bundles LLC + EIN + Mercury bank + Stripe US)

## Important pattern I noticed
**Two providers (eSIM Go, BNESIM) block UAE end-user purchases.** That's a real regulatory signal — TDRA's grip on UAE telecom is being enforced upstream. We need to specifically ask each new provider: *"Can your eSIMs be sold to customers physically located in the UAE?"* This question should go on the diligence questionnaire and Akito's notes.

Now to your question:

# How many providers did we check, and what did we learn about wholesale supplier's pricing?

## Providers — 8 total in the pipeline this session

| # | Provider | Account opened? | Real prices in hand? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | wholesale supplier Wifi | n/a (Akito's contact) | ✅ 9,091 SKUs in CNY |
| 2 | eSIM Access | ✅ Live | ✅ 2,808 SKUs in USD |
| 3 | eSIM Go | ✅ Live | ✅ 213 destinations — but UAE excluded |
| 4 | Telna | ✅ Live | ⚠️ Only 10 sample SKUs |
| 5 | 1GLOBAL | ⏳ Form sent today | ❌ Waiting 2–7 days |
| 6 | Airalo Partners | ⏳ Form sent today | ❌ 48hr verification |
| 7 | Nomad eSIM | ⏳ Form sent today | ❌ Sales follow-up pending |
| 8 | BNESIM | ⚠️ Email-only intake | ❌ Likely deal-breaker (UAE-block) |

So **3 providers with real price data**, **3 in flight** (will fire in the next week), **2 dead** (eSIM Go and BNESIM both block UAE buyers).

## wholesale supplier pricing insight — the original theory got *inverted*

Earlier I told you wholesale supplier was marking up eSIM Access by 40%. **The data didn't support that conclusion** once we actually held both price sheets side-by-side. Here's what we saw on the routes we could compare:

| Route | wholesale supplier (USD) | eSIM Access (USD) | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| USA 10GB / 7d | ~$3.61 | ~$3.80–4.20 | wholesale supplier cheaper |
| Thailand 5GB / 30d | competitive | similar/higher | tie |
| Several others | wholesale supplier equal-or-cheaper on most | — | — |

So the **40% flat margin** in wholesale supplier's sheet isn't a markup on top of eSIM Access — it's their internal **suggested retail vs settlement** spread (i.e. they're showing us *both* the wholesale price and the recommended retail price, and the gap is what they think we should keep). That's actually a normal aggregator pattern, not the smoking gun I first claimed.

## What's still suspicious about wholesale supplier

- CNY-denominated price sheet (not THB — they're Thailand-headquartered)
- 9,091 SKUs is too many to come from their own roaming deals
- Tier-2 aggregators usually have variable margins by carrier/region; wholesale supplier's spread is *too uniform*

→ Most likely interpretation now: wholesale supplier is a **legit reseller of a Chinese aggregator** (probably Redtea/eSIM Access *or* another CN-based player), with a sensible wholesale-to-retail spread. Not "ripping us off" — but also not the bottom of the supply chain.

## What this changes for the decision

Going wholesale supplier might actually be fine on price. **The real decision** is no longer "are we being overcharged?" — it's:

- Do we want Akito's relationship + smoother onboarding (wholesale supplier), **or**
- Direct upstream margin + no middleman risk (eSIM Access)?

That's a relationship-vs-margin trade-off, which is the conversation you and Akito actually need to have, not a math problem. Once the 3 pending providers (Airalo / Nomad / 1GLOBAL) come back with their net pricing, we'll have a much clearer picture of where wholesale supplier sits in the market.

## Turn 338 — Assistant

Cool. Brand strategy has four pieces to land: **positioning → audience → voice/tone → brand promise**. We'll go in that order — each one informs the next.

Starting with positioning. This is the question of *what makes Peanut eSIM different* from Airalo (the giant), Holafly (the unlimited-data brand), Saily (the NordVPN-backed newcomer), Nomad (the digital-nomad brand). Whatever we pick here shapes the logo, the copy, the marketing site, basically everything downstream.

Four directions I see for us, given who you are and who your seed users are:

| Angle | One-liner | Vibe |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Trust / local** | "The eSIM made by people who travel like you do" — leans on the UAE / Emirates-crew community origin | Warm, human, story-driven |
| **B. Aviation-first niche** | "Built for crew, used by everyone" — own the airline-staff niche before expanding | Insider, technical credibility |
| **C. Premium / minimal** | Apple-like polish, calm UX, fewer plans done better | Quiet luxury, design-led |
| **D. Honest / transparent** | "Real prices, no roaming theatre" — anti-fluff, show wholesale-style honesty | Direct, contrarian, witty |

My lean: **A or B**, because your seed-user advantage (Emirates crew + UAE-based travelers via Akito's network) is something *no other eSIM brand can copy*. C and D are positioning angles, but they're already crowded. A vs B comes down to how wide you want the brand to feel from day one.

# Which of these four resonates most — or is there a different angle we should be considering?

I don't know if it comes under these items or phases but let me just speak about what made me do this.

Okay so basically I am living in the UAE right now and so is my business partner Akito. ## Turn 349 — Assistant

Locked: **audience = cabin crew + cockpit crew (pilots), both from day 1.** That widens the design challenge a bit — cabin crew skew younger, more social, Facebook/Instagram-active; pilots skew older average, more professional. The voice has to work for both without alienating either.

So next sub-component: **voice & tone**. This is *how we sound* in every piece of copy — the app, push notifications, emails, support replies, social posts. Three plausible directions:

| Voice | Sounds like | Risk if we overshoot |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Insider-warm** | "Off to JFK on the 33rd? We've got you. 10GB activates the moment you land." | Too casual → pilots tune out |
| **B. Professional-direct** | "Activate 10GB in JFK before landing. No roaming charges." | Too corporate → cabin crew don't feel it's *theirs* |
| **C. Knowing-minimal** | "JFK · 10GB · activates on landing." (Confident, no fluff, lets the product speak) | Too cold → loses the community-by-crew feel that's our moat |

My lean: **A with restraint** — insider language and warmth, but never silly or memey. Apple-store-genius energy: friendly, informed, never patronizing. Works for both populations.

# Does A feel right, or do you want a leaner B/C tone?

## Turn 353 — Assistant

This is the real product. Honestly — I think this is *the* feature, not a feature. Everything we've been talking about (price wedge, voice, generic brand promise) is downstream of this. Let me say why, then re-pitch the brand promise around it.

**Why this is the actual moat:**

| Layer | What this idea unlocks |
|---|---|
| **Differentiation** | Crew SIM sells plans like everyone else. This collapses 5 plan-shopping decisions into one screenshot. Nobody else does this. |
| **Habit hook** | The 15th becomes "open Peanut day" every month — that's a permanent behavioral loop that no marketing budget can buy. |
| **Uses your proprietary data edge** | You already said earlier "we know what countries they go to, what data they use" — this turns that knowledge into a product feature instead of internal analytics. |
| **Sets up the Michelin layer naturally** | Once we have their schedule, we can surface "here are the crew-recommended places in LAX for your 22-hour layover" — the intelligence layer ships on top of the same data pipeline. |

**Tech feasibility (yes, park details for Phase 3 — but real high-level answer):**
Crew rosters from the Emirates Crew app have a predictable layout (date · flight number · route · times). A vision model (Google Gemini, GPT-4o, Claude — all of them handle this well today) can extract that into structured data in 1–2 seconds. The hard part isn't the OCR — it's the *pricing engine* that takes the parsed schedule and outputs the optimal plan bundle. That's where the cleverness lives, and it's totally buildable.

**The brand promise basically writes itself now:**

| Draft | The promise |
|---|---|
| **D** | *Screenshot your roster. We handle the rest.* |
| **E** | *Your schedule. Our plan. One tap.* |
| **F** | *Upload your roster on the 15th. Be connected for the next 30 days.* |

These all crush A/B/C from before because they describe a *specific thing* the product does, not a vague feeling. **My strong lean: D.** Six words. Says everything. Works as a homepage hero, an App Store subtitle, a poster in the crew lounge.

I want to save this as a project memory and queue it as a new Decision card on the discussion board (because it has real Akito-discussion + Phase 3 tech implications) before we move on.

## Item 4 — UI design system, your two questions

**Best design tool for an app like this:** Figma, by a huge margin. Industry standard, every freelancer / contractor / AI design tool (v0, Builder.io, Anima, Figma's own Make) plugs into it. **Figma is free** for individual use (unlimited personal files + 3 collaborative files in one team) — you only pay ($15/editor/month) when you add teammates. So zero cost to start.

**Best tool to actually build the app (Phase 3 territory, but answering now):**

| Stack | Fit for us | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **React Native + Expo** ⭐ | iOS + Android + web from one codebase. Best AI-coding ecosystem. | This is what we parked on earlier |
| Flutter | Good cross-platform, but worse AI tooling | Skip |
| FlutterFlow / Adalo | No-code, faster start | But you'll hit a ceiling fast |
| Native iOS (Swift) + Android (Kotlin) | Best quality, but 2× the work | Overkill |

Then layer Cursor + Claude Code + v0 / Builder.io on top of React Native for the AI-assisted "vibe coding" workflow. That's the modern indie/solo-founder stack and matches the budget you want.

---

## Item 5 — Key app screens, in depth

This is the v1 product map. Roughly 14 screens. Grouped by user journey:

| Group | Screens | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **First-run** | Splash · Onboarding (3 slides) · Sign-up · Crew verification | Get a verified Emirates crew member into the app |
| **Plan discovery** | Home (auto-plan via schedule upload OR manual browse) · Country list · Country detail · Plan detail | Browse + decide what to buy |
| **Purchase** | Checkout (Stripe) · Payment success · QR code + install instructions | Convert + deliver the eSIM |
| **My account** | My eSIMs · Active plan + data usage · Top-up · Travel history · Profile / settings | Manage what they already have |
| **Help** | FAQ · Contact support · Crew SIM-compatibility check | Reduce support load |
| **(Future — v1.x)** | Crew recommendations layer (the Michelin guide layer) | Goes here once the data pipeline is mature |

The **schedule-upload smart-plan** flow lives across the Home screen + a new "Upload roster" modal + an auto-generated Plan Summary screen. That's a 3-screen sub-flow added on top of the 14.

---

## Item 6 — Marketing website, in depth

This is **peanutesim.com** — the *public-facing* site, completely separate from the app. Three jobs:

| Job | What it does for us |
|---|---|
| **Discovery / SEO** | A crew member googles "Emirates crew eSIM" or "JFK eSIM cabin crew." Without a marketing site, we don't appear. Airalo has 200+ per-country landing pages for this exact reason. |
| **Conversion** | Someone hears about us in a Facebook group → opens the URL → sees the pitch → installs the app from a big button. |
| **Trust + legal** | About us, FAQ, blog, Terms / Privacy / Refund links. Apple + Google require a working website to approve an app. |

It's mostly content pages: home, "how it works," country pages, FAQ, blog, contact, legal. Built in Webflow or Framer (designer-led, no engineer needed) or Next.js (engineer-led, more flexible).

**The big strategic question for Item 6:** since we may go invite-only on the app, do we want the *marketing site* to be public (so search engines can find us, but it might leak the secrecy vibe) or gated behind crew login (max exclusivity, but invisible to SEO)? Probably hybrid — public site that *leads* to a "request invite" wall.

---

## Item 8 — clarification

I meant **transactional emails** (the boring but mandatory ones), not newsletters. There are two categories:

| Type | Examples | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Transactional** ⭐ mandatory | Sign-up confirmation · purchase receipt · eSIM delivery (QR + install instructions) · data-low warning · refund confirmation | Required for launch — Apple/Google + legal expect them |
| **Marketing / Newsletter** optional | Monthly digest · new destination launches · crew tips | Nice-to-have — can ship in v2 |

Templates get *designed* in Phase 2 (this item), then *populated by code* in Phase 3.

---

## Items 7 & 9 — noted

- **Item 7 (invite-only for v1):** This is a great instinct. It creates real exclusivity, drives FOMO in the crew Facebook groups, and side-steps Apple/Google moderation risk (because the eSIM market gets weird scrutiny). Park this as a working assumption.
- **Item 9 (social + ads + Facebook + WOM):** Solid. The brand guidelines doc still matters even with this approach — it's what keeps your ads consistent when you start running them.

| **5** | Soft Launch | Invite-only rollout to Emirates crew via Facebook groups + crew lounges, controlled growth, early feedback loop | ⚪ Not broken out yet |
| **6** | Public Launch & Growth | Open the gates, paid ads, ASO, referral programme, Etihad expansion conversation begins | ⚪ Not broken out yet |
| **7** | Scale & Strategic | Multi-airline (JAL, ANA, etc.), Michelin-recs layer goes mainstream, subscription model unlocks, possible adjacent products | ⚪ Not broken out yet |

Per the rule we set earlier — we only break out items for a phase once we start it. So Phases 0, 1, 2 are detailed; Phases 3–7 stay as stubs until we get to them.

| 9 | Android app (React Native + Expo) | Same codebase |

| 14 | Customer support tool | Intercom / Crisp / HelpScout |

| # | Item | What it is |

## Turn 400 — Assistant

# Crew SIM intel — the full profile

## Who they are

| Attribute | Finding |
|---|---|
| **Legal entity** | eSIM Mobile GmbH (German) |
| **Founder** | Michael Schoepfer — a real pilot, CEO & sole founder |
| **Team size** | "Team" page lists his name 5 times = likely solo with contractors |
| **Users (their claim)** | 5,000+ pilots and crew |
| **Coverage** | 180+ countries |
| **Airline partnerships (their claim)** | 30+ |
| **App age** | iOS v2.0.4 (last update 1 May 2026), 30 App Store ratings, 5.0★ |
| **Apps** | iOS + Android, English only, 52 MB |
| **Tagline** | *"The world's first eSIM built specifically for pilots and flight attendants"* |

## Pricing model — different from everyone

| Mechanic | What it means |
|---|---|
| **One global credit balance** | You top up €10/€50/€100 — that balance works everywhere |
| **Pay-per-MB** | Charged per actual usage, not per package |
| **No expiry** | Credit lasts forever |
| **No bundles** | No per-country plans, no time limits |
| **Referral baked in** | "Invite a colleague and both of you get free data" |

## Their rates for Emirates crew destinations

| Country | Crew SIM €/GB | Crew SIM $/GB equiv |
|---|---|---|
| **USA / UK / Thailand / Italy / Spain / Germany / HK / Russia / Greece / NZ / Norway / Belgium / Austria / Poland / Portugal / Ireland / Hungary / Denmark** | €1.43 | ~$1.53 |
| **France / Sweden / Switzerland / Czechia** | €1.33 | ~$1.43 |
| **Netherlands** | €1.23 | ~$1.32 |
| **Türkiye** | €1.02 | ~$1.10 |
| **Pakistan** | €1.13 | ~$1.21 |
| **Malaysia** | €1.54 | ~$1.65 |
| **Indonesia** | €1.64 | ~$1.76 |
| **Australia / Vietnam** | €2.05 | ~$2.20 |
| **Singapore** | €2.25 | ~$2.41 |
| **Saudi Arabia** | €2.97 | ~$3.18 |
| **South Africa / Bahrain** | €2.66 | ~$2.85 |
| **Egypt** | €2.76 | ~$2.96 |
| **India** | €3.17 | ~$3.40 |
| **South Korea** | €3.38 | ~$3.62 |
| **UAE (yes, they sell it!)** | €3.42 | ~$3.66 |
| **Brazil** | €3.07 | ~$3.29 |
| **Canada** | €3.58 | ~$3.84 |
| **China** | €4.40 | ~$4.71 |
| **Japan** | €4.61 | ~$4.94 |
| **Mexico** | €5.02 | ~$5.38 |

(Captured 41 destinations total — saved for memory.)

## Where they're strong (what we can't easily beat)

| Strength | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| **Real pilot founder** | Built-in credibility we don't have |
| **Pricing model is genuinely clever** | Single balance + no expiry feels modern |
| **B2B angle to airlines** | Has 30+ partners — would be hard to displace from those |
| **Sells UAE eSIMs** | They route around the eSIM Go / BNESIM UAE block somehow |
| **Referral mechanic baked in** | Already viral-loop optimized |

## Where they're weak (our day-1 wedge candidates, validated)

| Weakness | How Peanut wins |
|---|---|
| **No schedule-upload feature** | This is **the killer differentiator** — they make crew manually shop country-by-country every trip |
| **No crew-curated travel intel** | Our future Michelin layer has zero competition |
| **Generic global brand** | "By a pilot in Germany" doesn't beat "by a guy in your Dubai building who knows your crew" |
| **Solo team = slow product velocity** | 2 founders + AI tooling = faster shipping |
| **Pay-per-MB induces anxiety** | Pilots/cabin crew may prefer predictable per-trip pricing tied to their roster |
| **Marketing is generic** | Insta/X/FB/LinkedIn, paid ads. Not embedded in Emirates Facebook groups specifically |
| **B2B distraction** | They're chasing airline partnerships → slower consumer product velocity |

## Big strategic takeaway

The earlier **Decision 05** (day-1 wedge vs Crew SIM) leaned on *Better price*. **After this research, price is the wrong wedge** — they're already competitive with Airalo on most routes ($1.50-2/GB on big destinations). Trying to undercut by 10-20% won't move the needle.

**The real wedge is the schedule-upload smart-plan feature** — they don't have it, can't easily build it (solo team), and it's the thing that makes Peanut feel made-for-this-specific-job. We should update Decision 05's lean from "Better price" to **"Built around your roster, by people who know your airline."**

## Turn 411 — Assistant

# Item 6 — what it was about, in plain terms

**The question we asked:** *Crew SIM is the direct competitor to Peanut. Who exactly are they, how do they price, what are their strengths, and where can we beat them on day 1?*

## What we found

| Aspect | Finding |
|---|---|
| **Who they are** | A small German company (eSIM Mobile GmbH) founded by Michael Schoepfer, an actual pilot. Likely a solo or near-solo operation. |
| **How big** | ~5,000 users, 30 App Store ratings (5.0★), v2.0.4 of their app. Established but small. |
| **Coverage** | 180+ countries. They sell UAE eSIMs too (via du / Etisalat at ~$3.66/GB) — somehow routed around the regulatory block. |
| **Pricing model** | Different from Airalo/Holafly: one global credit balance, pay-per-MB, no expiry, no per-country plans to shop. Plus a referral mechanic. |
| **Actual rates** | About $1.50/GB on most major destinations (USA, UK, Thailand, most of Europe). Already competitive with Airalo — not expensive. |
| **B2B angle** | They court airlines directly with 30+ partnerships. Trying to be both consumer + enterprise. |

## What it means for Peanut

The single biggest takeaway: **price isn't the right way to beat them.** They're already at the Airalo level. If we try to undercut by 10-20%, they can just match us — and we'd be in a price war that hurts our margin without changing crew behavior.

**Where Peanut can actually win:**

| Their weakness | Peanut's wedge |
|---|---|
| No schedule-upload feature | The killer differentiator — you screenshot your roster, we generate the plan |
| No crew-curated travel recs | Our future Michelin-style layer |
| "Built by a pilot in Germany" | "Built by your guy in Dubai who knows your crew" |
| Solo team = slow shipping | 2 founders + AI tooling = faster product velocity |
| Generic ad marketing | Embedded in Emirates Facebook groups, word-of-mouth native |

## The strategic conclusion that came out of Item 6

We revised **Decision 05 (day-1 wedge against Crew SIM)** from your original lean of *"Better price"* to **"Schedule-upload smart-plan."** Working brand positioning candidate: *"Built around your roster, by people who know your airline."*

So Item 6 wasn't just research — it changed our positioning. Whatever we build in Phase 2 (brand strategy, app design, marketing site) and Phase 3 (the actual feature build) now points at the schedule-upload feature being THE thing, not a nice-to-have.

Next up was Item 10 (Risk register) — moving to that now.

## Turn 413 — Assistant

# Item 7 — Market sizing (TAM / SAM / SOM)

## Three layers — what each means

| Layer | Definition | For Peanut |
|---|---|---|
| **TAM** | Total Addressable Market — if you could magically reach 100% of everyone who could ever use your product | All airline crew (cabin + cockpit) globally |
| **SAM** | Serviceable Available Market — the realistic ceiling of who you could reach given your business model | Crew of airlines we can plausibly expand to over 3-5 years |
| **SOM** | Serviceable Obtainable Market — what you actually expect to capture in a defined window | Our 3-year crew-acquisition target |

---

## TAM — global airline crew

Sourced from IATA / ICAO industry data + airline employment numbers:

| Segment | Approx. global headcount |
|---|---|
| Cabin crew (worldwide) | ~1,200,000 |
| Commercial pilots (worldwide) | ~350,000 |
| **TAM (cabin + cockpit)** | **~1,550,000 crew globally** |

So the theoretical universe is ~1.5M people. Big enough to be interesting, small enough to know who they are by name through Facebook groups and unions.

---

## SAM — what's realistically reachable

Filtering down to airlines where we have a credible path (Akito's UAE network → Gulf → Asia → expand):

| Region / Airline group | Approx. crew |
|---|---|
| **Gulf carriers** (Emirates, Etihad, Qatar, Saudia, flydubai, Oman, Gulf Air) | ~70,000 |
| **Major Asian carriers** (Singapore, Cathay, JAL, ANA, Malaysia, Thai, Korean, Asiana) | ~80,000 |
| **European premium** (BA, Lufthansa, Air France-KLM, Swiss, KLM, Turkish) | ~110,000 |
| **North American majors** (Delta, United, American, Southwest) | ~110,000 |
| **Other** (Oceania, LatAm, Africa premium) | ~60,000 |
| **SAM total** | **~430,000 crew** |

So roughly a quarter of TAM is realistically serviceable for us over 3-5 years.

---

## SOM — Peanut's 3-year capture target

Working backward from a realistic adoption curve:

| Year | Phase / focus | Crew target | % of SAM | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Y1** | Soft-launch, Emirates only | 500 – 2,000 | 0.1 – 0.5% | 2–7% of Emirates crew. Realistic from Akito's seed network + Facebook group push |
| **Y2** | Expand Emirates + open Etihad + Qatar | 5,000 – 10,000 | 1 – 2.3% | Surpasses Crew SIM's current 5K user claim |
| **Y3** | All Gulf carriers + early Asian (Singapore, Cathay) | 20,000 – 40,000 | 5 – 9% | Real business at this scale |

### Revenue implications

Assuming average revenue per crew (ARPU) of **$15–$25/month** (crew fly multiple countries, mix of light + heavy travel months):

| Year | Mid-case crew | Mid-case ARR |
|---|---|---|
| Y1 | 1,000 crew | ~$240K |
| Y2 | 7,500 crew | ~$1.8M |
| Y3 | 30,000 crew | ~$7.2M |

So if Year-3 SOM lands mid-range, we're looking at a **$5-7M ARR business 3 years in**. Capital-light (no inventory, no warehouses, no physical SIMs), so most of that drops to operating margin once aggregator + Stripe fees are covered.

---

## What this tells us about the business

| Reality check | What it means |
|---|---|
| **TAM is real but not huge** | 1.5M is small relative to consumer eSIM (Airalo serves 20M+). This is a niche — but a *valuable* niche because the users have predictable, repeatable, monetizable travel patterns. |
| **Crew SIM at 5K = 0.3% SOM penetration** | They've barely scratched the market. Plenty of room. |
| **Gulf carriers are 16% of SAM** | Means Emirates → Etihad → Qatar → Saudia is roughly a $1M ARR opportunity by Y2 if executed well. |
| **The "yearly subscription" play really matters** | At 1.5M TAM, ARPU is everything. If we can convert crew from per-trip buying to a $30/mo flat plan, the business compounds dramatically. |
| **Defensible if we move fast** | A niche this small can be saturated by one strong brand. Crew SIM hasn't done it. The race is real. |

---

## Caveats (worth flagging)

- Crew numbers are estimates from public reports / Wikipedia / IATA — not exact. ±10% on most lines.
- ARPU assumption ($15-25/month) is roughly modeled on Crew SIM-like pricing × crew travel frequency. Could be higher with the smart-plan + yearly subscription, lower if heavy crew buy infrequently.
- SOM curve assumes we execute. Most startups in similar niches stall at 1-3K users. The Akito-Emirates network is what gives us a credible path to 20K+.

---

I'll add a TAM / SAM / SOM card to Phase 0 Item 7 on the live site (marking it CLOSED with today's findings) plus save the breakdown to project memory so it surfaces in any future session. The numbers will need a refresh quarterly as airlines report updated headcounts.

all the github things can we do it as sub agent so that it wont break my flow ?

## Turn 426 — Assistant

Yes — Agent tool exists exactly for this. Quick explanation:

## What a sub-agent is

A separate Claude instance I launch in the background. I hand it a self-contained brief (it can't see our conversation, so I have to spell out everything it needs to know), it works in parallel, and I get a result message when it's done — without it interrupting our chat.

## What it would actually do for us

I'd hand off all the **queued GitHub work** that's piling up:

| Queued work | Why it doesn't need your input |
|---|---|
| Phase 0/1/2 new item insertions (already in file, just needs verifying) | Already drafted |
| Decision 05 / 13 (and earlier 06-12) cards on the Open Discussion board | Already drafted |
| Phase 3-7 full detail sections (60 items I sketched in the audit) | I have the spec |
| Phase 0 Item 6 + Item 7 close-out tags | Mechanical |
| Top phase overview table count refresh | Mechanical |
| Copy 3 safe files to docs/downloads/ + add Downloads section to Documents tab | Mechanical |
| Final commit + push | Mechanical |

## Trade-offs to know

| Pro | Con |
|---|---|
| Doesn't break our flow — we stay on Phase 0 items | Agent can't ask me follow-up questions mid-task; I have to brief it well upfront |
| Runs in parallel — saves real time | If it makes a mistake, I'll only catch it when I look at the result |
| Notifies me when done | A push to your live GitHub Pages site is *not reversible* once live — I'll have the agent stop *before* the push and let me verify, then I'll do the push myself |

So my plan: **agent handles everything up to but NOT including the push**. It commits locally, I review the diff with you, you confirm, then push. That way nothing irreversible happens behind your back.

# Want me to launch the agent now with that scope — do everything but stop before the push?

Honest answer: **it's not too late, but I don't think a separate "plan file" would actually help much** — because the live site already IS the plan. The Roadmap tab has 102 items across 8 phases. The Open Discussion tab has 16 open decisions. The Cost tab has the financials. That's a more comprehensive plan than any single document would be.

What we've actually been doing is **rolling planning + immediate execution**:

| What we do | Why this works |
|---|---|
| Discuss a phase/item conversationally | You build understanding as we go, not as a wall of text |
| Surface decisions to the Open Discussion board | Captures everything for Akito review |
| Update the Roadmap as items close or items emerge | The site is a *living* plan, not a frozen one |
| Memory files capture strategic insights between sessions | So we don't repeat ourselves tomorrow |

The honest weakness of this approach: there's no **executive summary** — a 1-2 page "where Peanut is right now, what's open, what's next" that you could print and show Akito over coffee. Everything's spread across the live site.

So the real choice isn't *"plan first vs. ad-hoc"*. It's:

| Option | What it looks like |
|---|---|
| **A. Keep going as we are** (my lean) | Roll through Phase 0 items, agent handles GitHub in background, site stays the canonical plan |
| **B. Pause, agent writes a Master Plan doc** | 1-day detour to produce a single PLAN.md / PLAN.html that summarizes everything. Then we work from it. |
| **C. Both** | Keep going, but I have the agent produce a 1-page **Executive Summary** as one of its GitHub tasks. You get the bird's-eye view without us pausing the flow. |

My lean: **C**. You don't lose momentum, you get the bird's-eye doc for Akito conversations, and the live site stays the source of truth.

| 7 | Key-person risk — Akito leaves or shifts focus | His network is half the moat |
| 8 | Fraud / chargeback waves on Stripe US | High-risk MCC, eSIMs attract abuse |
| 9 | Stripe terminates our merchant account | Cascading shutdown — Stripe-dependent business |
| 10 | UAE residents-only marketing builds dependence on a single base | Hard to expand later if Emirates is 95% of users |

## Turn 442 — Assistant

Starting with **one example sale** so the math is concrete. Then we scale it up.

## Worked example: an Emirates cabin crew member buys a USA 10GB / 7-day plan

This is roughly the most common scenario — a layover in JFK, normal data needs for a week.

| Line | Web checkout | In-app purchase (IAP) |
|---|---|---|
| Retail price charged to crew | $13.00 | $13.00 |
| − wholesale supplier wholesale cost | −$3.61 | −$3.61 |
| − Stripe fee (2.9% + $0.30) | −$0.68 | (built into Apple cut) |
| − Apple 30% cut | — | −$3.90 |
| − Customer acquisition cost (low — FB groups + WOM) | −$2.00 | −$2.00 |
| **Net margin per sale** | **$6.71** | **$3.49** |
| **Margin %** | **52%** | **27%** |

A few things this tells us:
- **Web checkout almost doubles our margin** — confirms the "Netflix Hybrid" lean on Decision 01
- $6.71 net per sale is healthy for a $13 product
- Even the IAP-only worst case ($3.49) still has margin — we're not break-even-on-sale
- If wholesale supplier's wholesale drops or we pick a cheaper aggregator (eSIM Access direct), the $3.61 becomes ~$2.50 → margin climbs further

Two assumptions in this row that you and Akito would lock with Decision 10 (Plan & Pricing Strategy):
1. **Retail price = $13** — about 13% below Airalo's $15 for the same plan. Slightly aggressive but matches "modest profit, volume-led, first-choice" posture.
2. **CAC = $2** — assumes mostly organic acquisition (Facebook groups + word of mouth). If we run paid ads heavily, this could climb to $5-8.

## Item 9 — Financial runway model

The question: **how much capital, from $0 to break-even, and where does it come from?**

Headline answer first (then we can dig in):

| Stage | Spend | Cumulative |
|---|---|---|
| Phase 0-2 setup (LLC + trademark + ToS + design tooling) | ~$8K – $12K | $8K – $12K |
| Phase 3 tech build (you sweat-equity + AI tooling subscriptions ~$100/mo for 4 mo) | ~$2K | $10K – $14K |
| Phase 4 beta (TestFlight + low-scale ops costs over 2 mo) | ~$1K | $11K – $15K |
| Phase 5 soft launch (3-6 mo of burn before revenue covers it) | ~$2K – $4K | $13K – $19K |
| Safety buffer (3 months operating costs) | ~$2K | **~$15K – $21K total** |

**Time from incorporation to break-even: ~7–9 months**, assuming you and Akito are sweat-equity (not drawing salaries) and dev is AI-tool-driven (per Decision 07).

## Turn 449 — User

## Turn 450 — Assistant

Sure — let me explain it more concretely.

## What "budget ceiling" really means

It's not a *target spend* — it's a **stop-line**. The number where you say *"if we hit this and we still don't have a sustainable business, we pause and rethink (or raise outside money, or shelve the project)."*

Think of it like the fuel gauge on your car. The ceiling = the size of the tank. You don't *plan* to use the whole tank, but knowing the size is what tells you when you've gone too far without a station in sight.

## Concrete worked example with a $20K ceiling

| Step | Spend | Remaining |
|---|---|---|
| Start | — | $20,000 |
| Phase 1 (LLC + trademark + ToS) | -$4,000 | $16,000 |
| Phase 2 (design tools, domains, brand) | -$2,000 | $14,000 |
| Phase 3 (dev tooling subscriptions × 4 months) | -$2,000 | $12,000 |
| Phase 4 (beta, test eSIM purchases) | -$1,500 | $10,500 |
| Phase 5 (soft launch — burn before revenue covers it) | -$3,500 | **$7,000 left as buffer** |

So a $20K ceiling means: if at any point in Phase 5 we're approaching $13,000 spent and revenue still isn't covering monthly burn, the alarm bell rings — that's when you sit down with Akito and decide: cut scope? raise money? pause?

## Why the three options exist

| Option | What you give up | What you gain |
|---|---|---|
| **A — $15K (tight)** | Can't hire anyone, can't run paid ads | Lower personal risk, forces creative discipline |
| **B — $20K (Balraj lean)** | $5K more personal exposure | Headroom for one freelance contractor if AI tools fail you on one task, small paid-ads test |
| **C — $25-30K (generous)** | More personal exposure | Comfortable; can hire a designer if needed, real ad budget for soft launch |

We already decided no-freelancer-for-design (Decision 07). So the $20K ceiling assumes you might still need a contractor for some *other* bottleneck — maybe a backend integration that's too complex for AI tooling, or a paid-ads test in soft launch.

## The honest bottom line

The ceiling isn't a prediction. It's a **commitment device** you set with Akito *now* so neither of you can suddenly say "let's spend another $10K on this" in month 6 without it being a deliberate decision.

## Turn 454 — Assistant

## Decision 15 — how do you and Akito split the bankroll?

**The question, simply:** if Peanut needs $20,000 to get from $0 to break-even, who puts in how much?

## Why it matters beyond just the money

It's not just *who pays for what*. The capital split usually maps to **equity percentages** — i.e. who owns what % of the company. So this conversation is really about ownership, with money as the proxy.

Example with $20K bankroll:

| Split | Balraj puts in | Akito puts in | Implied equity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A. 50/50** | $10K | $10K | 50% each |
| **B. 60/40 (Balraj more time-intensive)** | $12K | $8K | 60/40 — Balraj does more dev/ops |
| **B. 40/60 (Akito brings more network)** | $8K | $12K | 40/60 — Akito's wholesale supplier contact + crew network has higher value |
| **C. Defer until founder agreement** | $10K each now | $10K each now | Sort out percentages later via Founder Agreement |

## Why this is tricky

This is the conversation founders most often **delay until it's painful**. Two reasons it's worth doing now, not later:

1. **The Delaware LLC structure currently has Balraj as sole owner** (single-member LLC, per Decision 00D). Akito is a co-founder *in spirit* but not legally yet. If you spend money and Peanut takes off, what's Akito's claim?
2. **Time vs. money equivalence.** Akito brings the wholesale supplier relationship + Emirates crew network. Balraj brings dev + ops time. These aren't equal-dollar-value contributions. The split needs to reflect what each person is actually putting in.

## My honest take

You probably **don't need to decide the exact percentages today**. But you should:
1. Both put in equal amounts upfront ($10K each)
2. Track everything in a simple shared sheet
3. **Have the founder-agreement conversation within 3 months of incorporation** — that's where the split actually gets formalized

So the lean for Decision 15 ends up being something like **Option C (defer) + commit to a deadline**. Otherwise it never happens.

## Cadence
- Reviewed **monthly** in a Balraj × Akito sync
- Each risk has an owner who tracks it
- New risks added as they emerge (Decision 04 — UAE compliance — was discovered today)

## What's NOT a risk on purpose
Things people often add but I'd leave off:
- "Competitor X enters the market" — that's just business. We track but don't fixate.
- "Recession" — too macro to mitigate at our stage.

## Turn 469 — Assistant

# Item 12 — EIN Registration Confirmation

**What it is:**
EIN = Employer Identification Number. It's the US federal tax ID for the LLC. Think of it as the Social Security Number, but for the business instead of a person. Every US LLC has one.

**Why we need it:**

| Used for | Without it |
|---|---|
| Opening Mercury US bank account | Can't open account |
| Opening Stripe US merchant account | Can't process payments |
| Filing the annual Form 5472 + 1120 | Can't file taxes |
| Signing contracts as the LLC | Have to sign as personal |

So without an EIN, the LLC technically exists on paper but can't *do* anything.

**How we get it:**
Stripe Atlas handles it automatically as part of the LLC filing process. We don't fill a separate form — Atlas talks to the IRS on our behalf. The EIN arrives in the Atlas dashboard typically 1-3 weeks after the LLC is filed.

**Why it's tracked as a separate item:**
Two reasons:
1. Sometimes the IRS delays the EIN issuance even when the LLC is registered. We need to confirm it actually arrives — not assume it did.
2. Every downstream Phase 1 item (Mercury, Stripe, Apple Dev, Google Play, Aggregator MSA) depends on having the EIN in hand. So we explicitly track it as a milestone.

**What you actually need to do:**
Nothing while Atlas is processing. Just check the Atlas dashboard once a week after filing and confirm the EIN appears. If it hasn't arrived after 4 weeks, contact Atlas support.

**Cost:** $0 — included with Atlas.
**Timeline:** 1-3 weeks after LLC filing (Item 1).

# Item 13 — Delaware Annual Franchise Tax + Report

**What it is:**
Every year, Delaware charges your LLC a **flat $300 fee** just for existing as a Delaware entity. It's not a tax on profits — it's a tax on the *right to be a Delaware LLC*. (If we'd incorporated as a Corporation, it'd be calculated on shares — for LLCs it's a clean flat number.) Plus a one-page "Annual Report" filing alongside the payment.

**Why it exists:**
This is how Delaware funds itself. Delaware has no state income tax for out-of-state companies (which is why we're using it), so the state collects revenue via these flat franchise taxes from every LLC registered there. Roughly 1.5 million Delaware entities × $300 = $450M/year for the state. It's the "rent" we pay to keep the LLC alive.

**The deadlines that matter:**

| Date | What happens |
|---|---|
| **1 June every year** | Annual report + $300 due |
| 1 June + 1 day late | $200 late penalty added (so total becomes $500) |
| Roughly 30 days late | LLC loses **"good standing"** status |
| Loss of good standing | Cascading damage (see below) |

**Why "loss of good standing" is the scary part:**

It's not just a paperwork issue. When a Delaware LLC loses good standing, real things break:

| What breaks | Practical impact |
|---|---|
| Mercury bank account | Frozen until restored |
| Stripe US merchant | May be suspended |
| Contracts signed by the LLC | Become technically unenforceable |
| Apple Developer / Play Console | Could be locked if Apple/Google check |
| Selling the company later | Can't transfer ownership of an LLC in bad standing |

So missing one $300 payment can effectively halt the business for weeks while you restore standing. This is why we explicitly track it.

**How to actually pay it:**

| Method | How |
|---|---|
| **Stripe Atlas autopay** ⭐ | Atlas can charge the LLC's payment method automatically each year. Recommended. |
| Manual via Delaware portal | Log into corp.delaware.gov each May, file + pay manually |
| Through registered agent | If you use a separate registered agent service, they often handle it for ~$50/yr extra |

**Recommendation:** Enable Atlas autopay + put a Google Calendar reminder on **15 May annually** (2 weeks before due date) as a backup. Costs $0 extra.

**Cost:** $300/year (forever, while Peanut exists as a Delaware LLC).
**Owner:** Balraj (this is part of the financial-system maintenance).

| **App build** (actual React Native code) | Phase 3 | Items 8 + 9 (iOS / Android) | Starts *after* Phase 2 designs are done. Realistic: **~4-6 weeks from now** if Phase 2 moves at pace. |

## The chronological flow

```
NOW                         Week 2-3              Week 6-8                Week 10+
─────                        ─────────             ─────────                ────────
Phase 1 admin items     →   Phase 2 brand     →   Phase 2 designs    →    Phase 3 build
(LLC, Stripe, EIN)          + logo + colors       (UI system, screens)    (RN code)
                                                                          
Done in parallel:           Locked: name,         Locked: full design     iOS + Android app
"system" plumbing           voice, palette         system + screens        ship to TestFlight
```

## Why not start the build right now

Three real reasons:

1. **No legal entity yet** — we can write code, but we can't sign the Aggregator MSA (need the LLC) or open the Stripe US merchant account (need EIN). So even if the app's done, nothing can actually transact.
2. **Designs feed the build** — building screens from a Figma design system is 3× faster than building + designing simultaneously. Skipping Phase 2 design always backfires.
3. **Brand isn't locked** — the logo, colors, and tone you pick in Phase 2 dictate every CSS variable and asset in the React Native code. Building first = rebuilding later.

## What we *could* do right now in parallel

While we're stuck on the name (Decision 00), there are 3 things that aren't blocked:

| Can start today | Why it's unblocked |
|---|---|
| Brand strategy conversation with Akito (Phase 2 Item 1) | Doesn't depend on name |
| **Schedule-upload smart-plan algorithm design** (Phase 3 Item 6 strategy work, not code) | Pure logic — doesn't need LLC or design system |
| Plan & pricing strategy with Akito (Phase 2 Item 10) | Akito's domain, doesn't depend on LLC |

So the answer is: **app design = Phase 2 (2-3 weeks out), app build = Phase 3 (4-6 weeks out)** — but there's productive work you and Akito can do in parallel right now that isn't gated on the name decision.

# Item 14 — US CPA Engagement

**What it is:**
A US Certified Public Accountant who handles your LLC's annual tax filings. Specifically the **Form 5472 + 1120 pro-forma** — the "we owe nothing, but we have to tell the IRS that" filing that every foreign-owned single-member LLC has to submit each year.

**Why it actually matters:**
The 5472 has a **$25,000 penalty** for missing or filing it incorrectly. It's the highest-risk admin item in the whole company. A wrong filing isn't a fine you negotiate down — it's $25K, full stop. So this isn't a "save money on accountants" line item.

**Why we need to engage one *before* incorporating:**

| Reason | Why |
|---|---|
| They review the structure during formation | Confirms we're setting up the LLC properly for tax-treatment-as-disregarded-entity |
| They set up the bookkeeping → tax mapping | Determines what we track in QuickBooks from day 1 |
| They become the named tax preparer | Some banks (Mercury) want a CPA on record |
| First tax year starts with incorporation | Filing happens within ~15 months of formation — too late to find someone in Month 14 |

**The real decision — four routes:**

| Option | Annual cost | Risk | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A. Stripe Atlas-partnered CPA ⭐** | $400–800 | Lowest — they know Atlas LLCs cold | Fast (Atlas hands you a vetted list) |
| **B. International-tax specialist** (Greenback, Bright!Tax, MyExpatTaxes) | $800–1,500 | Lowest — they specialize in non-resident-owned LLCs | Need to research + interview |
| C. Random local US CPA | $300–600 | High — most US CPAs don't know foreign-owned LLC rules → risk of $25K penalty | Slow + risky |
| D. DIY with software (FormBox, etc.) | $50–100 | Catastrophic — $25K penalty exposure | Fast but reckless |

**My honest recommendation: Option A (Atlas-partnered).**

Three reasons:
1. They've already done thousands of identical filings for Atlas-formed LLCs
2. Cost is reasonable ($400-800 is fine money compared to $25K penalty exposure)
3. Stripe will vouch for them — there's recourse if something goes wrong

Option B is the "safety upgrade" if you want the absolute best — but Atlas-partnered already handles this safely.

**The decision worth logging:**

| The choice | Status |
|---|---|
| Option A (Atlas-partnered) vs Option B (international specialist) | Lean: A. Worth logging as a Decision card for Akito's awareness (we're spending ~$600/year on a CPA before any revenue arrives). |

**Owner:** Balraj (this is financial-system maintenance).
**Cost:** ~$400-800/year (with Option A).
**Timeline:** Engage before LLC filing (Item 1).

## What this is, in 3 sentences

1. Every US company has to file paperwork with the US tax office every year, even if it made zero money.
2. For your specific case (a US company owned by you in UAE, no actual US business), there's special paperwork called Form 5472. If you mess it up or miss it: **$25,000 fine.**
3. So we need to hire an **accountant** to handle this paperwork once a year. That's all this item is.

## Just one decision you need to make

**Which accountant to hire — convenient option or specialist option?**

| Option | What it means | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Convenient (recommended)** | Stripe Atlas gives you a list of pre-vetted accountants who do this exact paperwork all day, every day. Pick one from their list. | ~$400-800/year |
| **Specialist** | Companies like Greenback or Bright!Tax that specialize in international tax. More premium, more expensive. | ~$800-1,500/year |

Both options are safe. The convenient one is cheaper and easier; the specialist is overkill for our situation.

## My recommendation

Go convenient — pick from the Stripe Atlas list. Costs ~$600/year, handles everything, low effort, no risk of the $25K fine.

## What you need to do (or not)

| Action | When |
|---|---|
| Decide which option to go with | Now (or whenever you and Akito sync) |
| Actually pick the specific accountant from the list | After the LLC is incorporated (so they can see the actual entity) |
| Sign their engagement letter | Once a year, before tax season |

That's it. Cost: ~$600/year. Owner: you. Akito doesn't need to do anything here.

## Turn 486 — Assistant

# Item 15 — Bookkeeping System

## What this is, in plain English

A tool that **automatically records every dollar in and out** of the business. It connects to Mercury (your bank) and Stripe (your payment processor) and keeps a running tally of: what you sold, what fees you paid, what expenses came up.

## Why we need it from day 1

Three reasons it can't be skipped:

| Reason | Why |
|---|---|
| **CPA needs it to file taxes** (Item 14) | Without clean books, the CPA can't file Form 5472. They'll charge 3× more to clean it up. |
| **Banks + investors check it** | Mercury, future investors, future acquirers — first thing they ask for is "show me your books" |
| **You need to know if you're making money** | At $0 you don't, but at $5K revenue/month you need to see profit, not just the bank balance |

Catching up later = expensive nightmare. Easier to start clean from day 1.

## The realistic options (just two worth your time)

| Option | Cost | What you give up |
|---|---|---|
| **A. QuickBooks Online (Simple Start) ⭐** | $30/mo (~$15/mo with Atlas discount Y1) | Nothing — industry standard, every CPA knows it |
| **B. Xero** | $13–70/mo | Slightly less universal, but cleaner UX |

I'm ignoring DIY spreadsheets (breaks at any scale) and Pilot/Bench (premium $200+/mo, overkill for our stage).

## My honest recommendation

**QuickBooks Online Simple Start.** Three concrete reasons:

1. The Atlas-partnered CPA you'll hire (Decision 16) almost certainly defaults to QuickBooks
2. Stripe Atlas typically gives ~50% off the first year as a partnership perk
3. If you ever sell the company, the acquirer's auditors expect QuickBooks

## What you actually need to do

| Action | When |
|---|---|
| Pick QuickBooks vs Xero | Now-ish (small decision) |
| Sign up + connect Mercury + Stripe | Right after incorporation |
| Set up account categories with the CPA | Same week as CPA engagement |

Ongoing effort: ~30 minutes a month to review + categorize transactions.

**Cost:** $15–30/mo ongoing.
**Owner:** Balraj.

# Item 16 — Apple Carrier Entitlement Application

## What this is, in plain English

Apple gives a special permission to certain eSIM apps that lets users install the eSIM **with one tap inside your app**. Without this permission, the user has to:

1. Save the QR code from your app
2. Go to iPhone Settings → Cellular → Add Cellular Plan
3. Scan the QR code
4. Confirm

With this permission ("Carrier Entitlement"), all that becomes a single button tap inside your app. Much smoother UX. **Airalo has it. Most newer eSIM apps don't.**

## Why we don't need it at launch

| Reality | Why it's fine |
|---|---|
| Apple doesn't grant this to brand-new apps | They want to see proven track record + serious user base first |
| Airalo didn't have it for its first ~2 years | They survived just fine with the QR-code flow |
| Users adapt — the QR flow is the eSIM industry standard | Not a deal-breaker |
| Applying too early = automatic rejection | Wastes the application attempt |

So skipping it at launch isn't a compromise — it's standard practice.

## When we actually apply

| Trigger | When |
|---|---|
| ~10,000 active users | Apple expects scale before granting |
| 6-12 months post-launch | Track record matters |
| Established App Store ratings (4.5★+ at scale) | Quality signal |

## What's already decided

This sits in **Open Discussion as Decision 02 — Carrier Entitlement**, with status "DEFERRED UNTIL SCALE." Already locked.

So Phase 1 Item 16 isn't a new decision — it's just a tracking item that reminds us to actually apply when the trigger conditions hit. The "decision" was made in Decision 02; Item 16 is the operational follow-through.

## What you actually need to do

Nothing right now. Set a future calendar reminder for **3 months post-launch** to check our active-user count + ratings, then we evaluate whether to apply.

**Cost:** $0 (Apple charges no fee for the application itself).
**Owner:** Balraj.
