## Turn 1954 — Assistant

# Section 5 — Operating Cost / User / Month

## What it is

Costs we pay **per active user, beyond wholesale data**. This hits gross profit directly. Currently $1.10/user/mo = $13.20/year per user.

## Current line items

| Line | $/user/mo | What it covers |
|---|---|---|
| Cloud / API per user | **$0.15** | Firebase, API gateway, server compute |
| Payment processing (2.9% + $0.30) | **$0.50** | Stripe/Apple IAP fees |
| Customer support amortized | **$0.30** | CX salaries / tools / per-ticket cost |
| SMS / OTP / verification | **$0.05** | Twilio for sign-up verification |
| Hosting + dev tools amortized | **$0.10** | GitHub, monitoring, deploy infra |
| **TOTAL OPEX / user / month** | **$1.10** | |

# 🚩 Three things worth questioning

## 1. Payment processing might be UNDER-estimated

Stripe charges **2.9% + $0.30 per transaction**.

| Scenario | Real cost |
|---|---|
| One sub charge / month at $17.99 (Premium) | $0.82 |
| One sub + 1 refuel ($17.99 + $4.99) | $0.97 |
| Annual Cockpit charge ($299) | $8.97 — but spread over 12 months = $0.75/mo |
| Blended across all users | **~$0.80** (not $0.50) |

**Should bump to $0.80** — a $0.30 increase per user. At 100K users = $30K/mo extra cost we'd be missing.

## 2. Customer support is probably WRONG at scale

| Phase | Realistic cost |
|---|---|
| Pre-launch (months 0-6) | ~$2-3/user (1 founder doing it all) |
| Early scale (10K users) | ~$0.50/user (1-2 CX hires) |
| Mature (100K+ users) | ~$0.15-0.20/user (Intercom AI + 5-6 humans) |

Currently flat $0.30 — doesn't reflect the **curve**. We should add a phase-based model.

## 3. What's MISSING entirely from this section

These aren't per-user costs but **fixed monthly burn** we'll need to model elsewhere:

| Cost | Estimate / month |
|---|---|
| Founder salaries (Balraj + Akito) | $0-15K (depends if drawing salary) |
| Legal / accounting | $1-2K |
| Insurance (E&O, cyber, liability) | $300-500 |
| Marketing (post-launch) | $500-5K |
| Office / co-working (if any) | $0-2K |
| Software stack (Slack, Notion, etc.) | $200-500 |
| **Total fixed monthly burn** | **~$2K-25K depending on scale** |

## My recommendation — three changes

| Change | Action |
|---|---|
| Bump payment processing to **$0.80** (real Stripe blended) | Update $0.50 → $0.80 |
| Replace flat customer support with **phase-based** | Add scenario column (pre-launch / early / mature) |
| Add a **separate "Fixed Monthly Burn"** section | New table for non-per-user costs |

After: total OPEX/user/mo becomes **~$1.40** (current $1.10 + payment fix). Plus fixed burn modeled separately.

## At scale impact

| Users | Current OPEX (× $1.10) | Corrected OPEX (× $1.40) | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10,000 | $11,000/mo | $14,000/mo | +$3K/mo |
| 100,000 | $110,000/mo | $140,000/mo | +$30K/mo |

Real OPEX is ~27% higher than the model shows.

## Turn 1958 — Assistant

# Debate: Change #1 — Payment processing $0.50 → ?

## The case for changing it (with real math)

Stripe charges **2.9% + $0.30 per transaction**. Per-user payment cost depends on:
- ARPU (tier they're on)
- # of transactions per month (subscription + refuels)

| Tier | Transactions/mo | $/transaction | Cost/user/mo |
|---|---|---|---|
| Economy PAYG (2.5 × $4.99) | 2.5 | $0.44 each | **$1.10** ← surprise |
| Roster Bundle ($19.95) | 1 | $0.88 | $0.88 |
| Premium Economy ($17.99 + maybe refuel) | 1.3 | $0.74 | $0.96 |
| Business ($29.99 + maybe refuel) | 1.25 | $1.03 | $1.29 |
| First ($39.99) | 1 | $1.46 | $1.46 |
| First Suite ($49.99) | 1 | $1.75 | $1.75 |
| Cockpit ($299/yr ÷ 12) | 0.08 | $9.97 | $0.74 |

**Blended weighted by current mix: ~$1.00/user/mo, not $0.50 or $0.80.**

The $0.30 fixed fee is brutal for Economy's small frequent transactions.

## The case AGAINST changing it

| Counter-argument | My response |
|---|---|
| "$0.50 is fine as a round number, just a placeholder" | True, but it's understating by ~50%. Multiplied across 100K users = $50K/mo missed cost. |
| "We don't know Stripe vs Apple IAP mix yet" | True — Apple is WORSE (30% for iOS subscriptions). If iOS-heavy, real cost could be $2-3/user. |
| "Will optimize at scale (bulk discounts)" | Stripe Volume tier kicks in at $80K/mo processed — saves ~0.4%. Still meaningful but $$ later. |
| "Some users don't pay" (chargebacks, fraud) | Adds ~1-2% to costs, not subtracts. |

## The actually-correct answer depends on iOS payment routing

**Big question — which one are we committing to?**

| Option | Payment processing/user/mo |
|---|---|
| **A — Stripe only** (web checkout, no in-app purchase) | **~$1.00** |
| **B — Apple IAP iOS + Stripe web/Android** (Apple charges 30%) | **~$2.50** (year 1), ~$1.50 (year 2+) |
| **C — Stripe + Apple optional** (route iOS users to web checkout via Universal Link) | **~$1.20** (some iOS users still use IAP) |

Apple requires apps to offer IAP if they sell in-app. We can DIRECT users to web for first purchase (legal post-2024) but iOS users who use the app for renewal often default to IAP. Realistic blended: ~$1.20-1.50.

## My honest recommendation: **$1.20**

| Why | |
|---|---|
| Reflects real Stripe + some Apple IAP exposure | |
| Conservative without over-engineering | |
| At 100K users = $120K/mo, ~$1.44M/year cost — material number worth getting right | |
| If we end up Stripe-only, model is slightly conservative (good for investors) | |

# Lock payment processing at $1.20/user/mo, or push back?

## Turn 1960 — Assistant

My bad — got too deep into the math. Let me reset.

# What this actually is — in one line

When a user pays us $17.99 for Premium, **we don't get the full $17.99**. Stripe (or Apple) takes a small piece. **You don't charge the user extra** — it just comes out of OUR pocket.

# A real example

| | $ |
|---|---|
| User's credit card is charged | $17.99 |
| Stripe's fee (2.9% + $0.30) | $0.82 |
| **What we actually receive** | **$17.17** |

That $0.82 is a **cost to us**, just like rent or salaries. It needs to be in the P&L.

# What's in the model right now (the placeholder)

The README says we lose **$0.50 per user per month** to payment fees.

I'm saying that's wrong — it's actually closer to **$1.20 per user per month** because:
- Stripe takes a cut on every transaction
- Some users (Economy PAYG) buy 2-3 times per month → 2-3 fees per month
- Apple App Store takes WAY more (30%) if users buy through iOS app

# What needs your attention

**Just one decision:**

> Do we update the model from $0.50 → $1.20 per user per month for payment fees?

That's it. Not asking you to charge the user differently. Not changing pricing. Just updating an internal cost line.

# Why it matters

At 100K users:
- Old assumption: $0.50 × 100K = $50K/month in fees
- Real assumption: $1.20 × 100K = $120K/month in fees
- **The model is missing $70K/month of cost = $840K/year**

If we don't fix this, our profit projections are inflated.

# Make sense now? Update the line to $1.20 or want me to explain a different part?

## Turn 1964 — Assistant

Honest answer: **$1.20 is a calculated estimate, not a hard fact.** Here's what's certain vs what I estimated.

# What's CERTAIN (published rates)

| Provider | Fee |
|---|---|
| Stripe (US cards) | 2.9% + $0.30 per transaction |
| Apple App Store (IAP) | 30% on year 1 / 15% year 2+ |
| Apple Small Business Program | 15% if under $1M revenue |

# What I ESTIMATED

| Variable | My guess | Confidence |
|---|---|---|
| Average transactions per user/mo | 1.3 | Medium |
| % of payments via Stripe vs Apple IAP | 70/30 | **Low** — guessing |
| Avg refuel attach rate per month | 30% | Medium |

# Range of realistic values

| Scenario | $/user/mo |
|---|---|
| **All Stripe (web checkout only, no in-app)** | **~$1.00** |
| **Mixed Stripe + Apple IAP 70/30** ⭐ I picked this | **~$1.20** |
| **Apple IAP heavy (50/50)** | **~$1.50** |
| **iOS-app-purchase heavy (most users buy in iOS app)** | **~$2.00** |

# Why I picked $1.20

I assumed:
- iOS users mostly check out via WEB initially (we route them via Apple Universal Link — locked decision)
- Some iOS users still default to in-app subscription renewal (~30%)
- Android + web users 100% on Stripe

If we get the routing right (web-first for iOS), real cost is ~$1.00.
If iOS users mostly stay in-app, cost balloons to $1.50-2.00.

# My honest call

| Option | Value |
|---|---|
| **$1.00** | Optimistic, assumes Stripe-only |
| **$1.20** | Realistic mixed (my recommendation) |
| **$1.50** | Conservative — protects against bad iOS routing |

For an investor pitch I'd use **$1.50 (conservative)**. For internal planning, **$1.20**.

# Quick verdict

**Above-average co-founder for this venture. Strong on strategy and detail. Weak on execution velocity and assumption validation. You're 8+ hours into perfecting a P&L model with no LLC filed, no domain registered, no crew interviewed yet — that's the pattern I'd push hardest on.**

# What you're doing genuinely well

| Strength | Evidence |
|---|---|
| **Adversarial product thinking** | You spotted the Economy → subscriber backdoor unprompted. Senior PMs don't catch that on day one. |
| **Brand sensitivity** | Caught my "Cockpit = pilots" misread instantly. Reframed it to "you be the pilot of your own data." That's CEO-level brand instinct. |
| **Detail orientation** | Caught 50% × 1mo vs 2mo carry error, the 8 GB Business pool inconsistency, the premature locks. Without you the model would have shipped wrong. |
| **Speed of decision** | When given choices ($29.99 vs $24.99, A vs B vs C), you pick. Don't agonize. CEO trait. |
| **Capital efficiency** | You're using sub-agents, building HTML deliverables, parallelizing work. Founder-economy thinking baked in. |
| **No ego** | Asked for feedback. Accepted "I made up 75/20/5" without flinching. Coachable. |
| **Co-founder discipline** | Knows what to delegate to Akito vs do yourself. That partnership maturity is rare in first-time founders. |

# Where you're slipping — brutal version

| Weakness | What I've observed |
|---|---|
| **Planning > Doing** | You've built 16 tabs of P&L modeling. You haven't filed the Delaware LLC, registered moonlight.ventures, or sent the wholesale supplier email. The model can wait. The market won't. |
| **Conceptual gaps you should have closed** | "What is Economy plan?" "Where did $4.99 come from?" These should have been written down in your own notes before our session. Re-asking foundational questions wastes founder time. |
| **No deadlines** | Every todo is "pending." Pre-launch ventures die from "I'll get to it." Convert "register domain" → "by Friday 6pm." |
| **Sometimes too quick to accept my call** | You locked $29.99 Business after I lobbied. A great CEO sometimes pushes back: "show me option B in more detail." You only did that on Cockpit. |
| **Validation gap** | We've designed an entire pricing ladder on assumptions about crew behavior. Akito hasn't interviewed a single crew member this session. The model could be defending a fiction. |
| **Voice-to-text noise** | Many garbled messages slow us down. Investor calls are unforgiving. Consider keyboard for the high-stakes stuff. |
| **Context-switching** | Tab 1 → backdoor → Sheet sync → Cockpit → tab scroll → Blueprint → back. We could have shipped 30% more in a focused linear pass. |

# Big-picture observations

| Observation | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| You're optimizing pricing while ignoring acquisition | Your gross margin is at 60-74% across tiers. Healthy enough. The real lever is whether 5,000 crew sign up. That's an Akito problem, not a P&L problem. |
| Akito's deliverable stack is growing | Crew interviews, brand decisions, roster validation, beta recruitment — all on him. Stress-test his bandwidth. |
| You have tech depth, growing finance depth, weak marketing intuition | Lean on Akito harder for go-to-market. Don't let your spreadsheet instinct creep into copy/positioning. |
| First-time-founder rookie tells: re-asking basics, perfectionism, over-modeling | But you compensate with detail orientation and partnership discipline. Net: still net-positive trajectory. |

# Tab 2 — User Behavior

## Bite 1 — Why this tab exists

Think of the user-behavior tab as the **prediction engine**. It takes one question and answers it:

> *Given that a crew member buys a plan, **how much data will they actually use** — and how often will they refuel?*

Every downstream tab needs to know this to compute COGS (we pay our supplier per GB consumed, not per GB sold).

# Tab 2 in two layers

| Layer | What it does |
|---|---|
| **Layer 1 — Archetypes** (rows 5-11) | Defines 4 generic user types: Light / Average / Heavy / Churner. Each has a % of pool consumed + refuel attach rate. |
| **Layer 2 — Per-tier consumption** (rows 13-24) | Applies a tier-specific consumption % to each plan's pool. Computes GB consumed, GB carried, GB breakage. |

The big change we just made: **Layer 2 now uses per-tier rates that override Layer 1's single weighted average.** Why? Because the 1-GB refill UX means different tiers consume differently — a 10 GB Premium user behaves differently than a 40 GB First Suite user, even if they're both "Average archetypes."

# What's NEW from when we first discussed Tab 2

| Then (start of session) | Now |
|---|---|
| Single 62.5% consumption applied to ALL tiers | Per-tier rates (40-100%) reflecting 1-GB friction reality |
| 6 tiers + refuel attach | 7 tiers (added First Suite) + refuel attach |
| Tab 2 fed the model with one weighted-average number | Tab 2 feeds tier-specific consumption rates |

# Ready for Bite 2 — the 4 archetypes (Light / Average / Heavy / Churner)?

## Turn 1992 — User

## Turn 1993 — Assistant

# Bite 2 — The 4 archetypes

Every paying crew member falls into one of four buckets, regardless of which tier they're on:

| Archetype | % of users | % pool consumed | Refuel attach | Mental picture |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Light** | 25% | 30% | 10% | "Subscribed Premium just in case." Pool sits mostly unused. |
| **Average** | 40% | 65% | 30% | Default crew. Eats most of pool. Refuels ~1 month in 3. |
| **Heavy** | 30% | 95% | 65% | Long-haul + connectivity dependent. Eats nearly everything, often refuels. |
| **Churner** | 5% | 10% | 0% | One month, cancels. Pure single-month breakage. |

Total = 100%. Weighted average pool consumption = 62.5%.

# Important — this is "framework," not the active driver anymore

When we first built Tab 2, that **62.5%** weighted number was used for *every* tier. But after we locked the **1-GB refill UX**, that broke — because friction makes a Premium user behave differently than a First Suite user even when they're both "Average archetypes."

So the archetypes now serve two purposes:

| Purpose | Who uses it |
|---|---|
| **Conceptual model** — describe who our users are (great for crew interviews + sensitivity tests) | Akito uses this when validating crew profiles |
| **Sensitivity input** — Tab 7 stress-tests by shifting the mix (what if Heavy goes from 30% to 45%?) | Investor analysis / what-if questions |

The **actual consumption math** for revenue/COGS uses the per-tier rates we set in Layer 2 (40% Premium, 45% Business, 54% First, 33% Suite, 60% Cockpit, 100% PAYG).

# Why crew-skewed (25/40/30/5) instead of general travel (40/35/20/5)?

| | General travel SaaS | Crew-skewed (ours) |
|---|---|---|
| Light | 40% | **25%** ↓ |
| Average | 35% | **40%** ↑ |
| Heavy | 20% | **30%** ↑ |
| Churner | 5% | 5% |

Reasoning: **crew fly 8-12 layovers/month as their job.** They're heavier users than typical travelers. The bell curve shifts right. We're modeling truck drivers, not vacationers.

# Bite 3 — Per-tier consumption section (rows 13-24)

This is where the math actually happens. For each tier, the table computes 5 things:

| Column | What it shows |
|---|---|
| **Pool GB** | Pulled from README — how much pool the user paid for |
| **% Consumed** | Per-tier friction-adjusted rate (NEW — was a single weighted avg before) |
| **GB Consumed** | Pool × % Consumed = what we pay supplier for |
| **GB Carries** | Unused × carry % = liability to next month |
| **GB Breakage** | Unused × (1 − carry %) = pure profit, never paid out |

## Worked example: Business tier

User pays $29.99 for 15 GB pool. Friction-adjusted consumption: **45%**. Carryover: **70% × 1 month**.

| Step | Math | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Pool | (from README) | 15 GB |
| GB Consumed | 15 × 45% | **6.75 GB** |
| GB Unused | 15 − 6.75 | 8.25 GB |
| GB Carries | 8.25 × 70% | 5.78 GB (banked) |
| **GB Breakage** | 8.25 × 30% | **2.48 GB** (gone — pure profit) |

So out of 15 GB Business sold:
- We pay supplier for **6.75 GB**
- User has 5.78 GB banked for next month
- We keep the revenue on **2.48 GB of breakage**

That breakage is the magic. Even if we sold the GB at cost, we'd still make money on the gap.

# Bite 4 — How the per-tier consumption %s differ and why

This is the most interesting part of Tab 2 — explaining why each tier consumes a different % of its pool.

## The locked per-tier rates

| Tier | Pool | Consumption | GB consumed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Economy (PAYG) | 1 GB | 100% | 1 GB |
| Roster Bundle | 5 flights | 100% | 5 flight SIMs |
| **Premium Economy** | 10 GB | **40%** | 4 GB |
| **Business** | 15 GB | **45%** | 6.75 GB |
| **First** | 25 GB | **54%** | 13.5 GB |
| **First Suite** | 40 GB | **33%** | 13.2 GB |
| **Cockpit** | 12 GB | **60%** | 7.2 GB |
| Refuel | 1 GB | 100% | 1 GB |

## Why these specific numbers — the friction logic

The **1-GB refill UX** caps actual consumption at roughly **13-14 GB per month** for most users, no matter how big the pool. So:

| Tier | Why this % |
|---|---|
| Premium Economy 40% | 10 GB pool. User wants ~4-6 GB, friction caps usage. Most users don't fully tap their pool. |
| Business 45% | 15 GB pool. Slightly heavier users, same friction. 6-7 GB consumed. |
| First 54% | 25 GB pool. Heavy users push more — they actually NEED more data. Hit ~13.5 GB. |
| First Suite 33% | 40 GB pool. Same heavy users (~13 GB consumed), but pool is huge so % drops. |
| Cockpit 60% | 12 GB pool. Smaller pool → less buffer → users use most of it. Plus emergency bank is separate. |

## The key insight (most important business lesson)

**Pool expansion is essentially free to us.**

Look at First (25 GB → 54%) vs First Suite (40 GB → 33%). Both consume ~13 GB absolute. Going from 25 to 40 GB pool:
- COGS stays the same (we pay for ~13 GB consumed either way)
- Perceived value improves dramatically ($1.60/GB → $1.25/GB)
- More users upgrade to Suite because it "looks like a better deal"

That's why First Suite is the anchor product — same cost to us, better marketing value.

## The honest caveat (flagging for blueprint)

These per-tier %s are **MY ESTIMATES** based on the 1-GB friction logic. Real consumption could land anywhere from 30-70% depending on:
- How prominent we make the refill button
- How aggressive the day-25 reminder is
- Whether crew share tips about "always refill" in WhatsApp
- Marketing messaging (e.g., "use it before you lose it")

Post-launch app analytics will replace these estimates with real numbers in 60-90 days.

# Your understanding cleaned up

| Your claim | Reality |
|---|---|
| Consumption ~same regardless of pool size | ✅ Correct (friction caps at ~13 GB) |
| Bigger pool = more unused = more breakage | ✅ Correct |
| We don't pay supplier for unused = pure profit | ✅ Correct |
| Pushing First Suite generates more revenue | ✅ **Yes — but the lever is profit, not just revenue.** |

# Small clarification on the last point

When a user buys First Suite ($49.99) instead of Business ($29.99):

| | Business | First Suite | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue | $29.99 | $49.99 | **+$20** |
| GB consumed (friction-capped) | ~6.75 GB | ~13.2 GB | +6.5 GB |
| COGS | $8.10 | $15.84 | +$7.74 |
| **Net profit per user** | **$21.89** | **$34.15** | **+$12.26** |

So each upgrade from Business to First Suite = **+$12.26/user/month pure profit**.

At 100K users with mix change of just 2% (2,000 users upgrade) = **+$294K/year**.

# The complete picture

Yes — **the strategic play is to push users up the ladder.** Three reasons compound:

1. **More revenue per user** (higher tier price)
2. **Same friction-capped COGS** (consumption doesn't scale with pool)
3. **More breakage = more pure profit margin**

This is why First Suite is the "anchor product" — best per-GB price draws upgrades, but our profit per user is also the highest.

## Turn 2005 — Assistant

# Bite 5 — The breakage column (where profit appears in numbers)

## What "breakage" actually is

Breakage = **GB the user paid for, but never used.** Once it expires (no carryover), we keep the revenue but never pay our supplier for it. **100% margin on those GB.**

## Per-tier immediate breakage (month 1)

| Tier | Pool | Consumed | Unused | Carries | **Breakage** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Premium Economy** | 10 GB | 4 GB (40%) | 6 GB | 3 GB (50%) | **3 GB** ⭐ |
| **Business** | 15 GB | 6.75 GB (45%) | 8.25 GB | 5.78 GB (70%) | **2.48 GB** |
| **First** | 25 GB | 13.5 GB (54%) | 11.5 GB | 11.5 GB (100%) | **0 GB** |
| **First Suite** | 40 GB | 13.2 GB (33%) | 26.8 GB | 26.8 GB (100%) | **0 GB** |
| **Cockpit** | 12 GB | 7.2 GB (60%) | 4.8 GB | 4.8 GB (100%) | **0 GB** |

## Wait — First, Suite, Cockpit show ZERO immediate breakage?

Yes. Because they all have **100% carry × 2 months**. That means:
- Month 1: 100% of unused rolls forward → no immediate breakage
- Month 2: same logic, but stuff from Month 1 starts to age out
- **End of Month 2: Month 1's unused GB expires → that's when breakage hits**

So for top tiers, breakage is **deferred by 2 months**. Tab 10 (Carryover Liability) tracks this aging.

## The trade-off pattern

| Tier | Carry | Immediate breakage | Deferred breakage | Stickiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Premium Economy | 50% × 1mo | **HIGH** (50% of unused) | Some | Low |
| Business | 70% × 1mo | Medium (30%) | Some | Medium |
| First / Suite / Cockpit | 100% × 2mo | **ZERO** | Eventually all unused | **High** |

**Higher tiers trade short-term breakage for long-term stickiness.** Crew feel like they're banking real data → they renew. The breakage eventually comes through carryover aging.

## In dollars — what breakage means for revenue

Wholesale blended at ~$1.20/GB. So 3 GB of Premium Economy breakage = **$3.60 of pure-margin profit per Premium user per month** that's literally money we never paid out.

At scale:
- Premium Economy users (13% × 100K) = 13,000
- 3 GB breakage × $1.20 = $3.60/user/mo
- **$46,800/month = $562K/year in pure breakage profit** from Premium Economy alone

Apply same logic to all tiers → breakage is one of the biggest profit levers in the model.

## Why ethics matters here

Breakage is ONLY ethical when:
- Pool is always visible (user sees what they paid for)
- Refill button is one tap away
- Day-25 reminders are honest
- No hidden mechanics

The locked transparency policy means **breakage we earn is from forgetfulness, not deception.** This was your call earlier — and it's the right one.

## Turn 2009 — Assistant

# Bite 6 — How Tab 2 feeds the rest of the model

This is the final piece. Tab 2 produces 4 outputs that get consumed by 7+ other tabs.

## Tab 2's outputs (the levers)

| Output | Where it lives | What it represents |
|---|---|---|
| **Per-tier consumption %** | Column C, rows 17-24 | How much of pool each tier consumes (40%/45%/54%/33%/60%) |
| **GB Consumed per user** | Column D | Actual GB we pay supplier for |
| **GB Carries** | Column E | Liability — user can spend next month |
| **GB Breakage** | Column F | Pure-margin profit |
| (Layer 1) Weighted archetype mix | Rows 6-11 | Still used for sensitivity testing in Tab 7 |

## Which tabs consume Tab 2's outputs

| Downstream tab | Uses Tab 2 for... | Produces |
|---|---|---|
| **Tab 3 — Per-Tier Unit Economics** | GB Consumed → COGS per user | Gross margin per tier |
| **Tab 4 — Pricing Simulator** | Behavior inputs | Break-even / margin-target retail prices |
| **Tab 5 — Volume Scenarios** | Per-user economics × user counts | 1K / 5K / 10K / 100K P&L |
| **Tab 7 — Behavior Sensitivity** | Archetype mix (rows 6-11) | What-if mix scenarios |
| **Tab 8 — LTV-CAC + Churn** | Consumption + churn | Lifetime value per tier |
| **Tab 10 — Carryover Liability** | GB Carries (column E) | Deferred revenue + aging schedule |
| **Tab 11 — Strategic Scenarios** | Behavior under different supplier paths | wholesale supplier vs direct comparison |
| **Tabs: Economy / Roster / Premium / Business / First / Suite / Cockpit Logic** | Per-tier consumption + behavior | Plan-specific deep-dive economics |

## The flow visually

```
Tab 1 (prices, pools, costs)  ─┐
                                ├──→  Tab 2 (User Behavior)  ──→  Tabs 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 10, 11 + Logic tabs
README inputs                  ─┘
```

Tab 2 is the **intelligence layer** — it transforms raw inputs (prices, pools) into behavioral predictions (consumption, breakage). Without Tab 2, the model has no idea what users will actually do.

## Why this matters operationally

**If you change ANY single number in Tab 2 — say bump Premium Economy consumption from 40% to 50% — the entire workbook recalculates:**

| Cascade effect | Direction |
|---|---|
| GB Consumed goes up | +25% |
| COGS goes up (Tab 3) | +25% |
| Breakage goes down (Tab 2) | -25% |
| Per-tier margin drops (Tab 3) | -3-5 percentage points |
| 24-month rollout shows lower profit (Tab 6) | -material |
| LTV drops (Tab 8) | -material |
| Investor pitch numbers worse | -confidence |

**That's why Tab 2 is the most leveraged tab in the workbook.** A small change here ripples everywhere.

## The wrap-up

Tab 2 is the **factory floor**. Every other tab is a different report from the same factory. Your job as founder is to keep these per-tier rates **honest** — neither too optimistic (overstates profit) nor too conservative (understates the business).

The current rates are MY estimates pending validation. Once Akito interviews crew and we get real usage data post-launch, these get replaced with measured values and the model becomes truly predictive.

# Tab 3 — Per-Tier Unit Economics

## Bite 1 — What Tab 3 does

In one line:

> *For each tier, computes how much money one user makes us per month — revenue minus all costs.*

Tab 1 + Tab 2 told us **prices** and **consumption**. Tab 3 puts them together to answer:

| Question Tab 3 answers |
|---|
| If I sell a **Premium Economy** subscription for $17.99, what's my profit? |
| If a crew member upgrades from **Business** to **First Suite**, how much more do we make? |
| Which tier has the highest **margin per user**? |
| Are we losing money on any tier? |

# How Tab 3 sits in the model

```
Tab 1 (prices)   ─┐
                  ├──→  Tab 3 (per-user economics)  ──→  Tab 5 (Volume Scenarios)  
Tab 2 (behavior) ─┘                                  └──→  Tab 6 (24-Month Rollout)
                                                     └──→  Tab 8 (LTV-CAC)
```

Tab 3 is **per-user math**. Multiply by user count → you get total revenue/profit.

# Ready for Bite 2 — the 13 columns explained?

## Turn 2022 — Assistant

# Bite 2 — The 13 columns explained

Grouped into 3 logical chunks so it's not overwhelming:

## Group 1 — Inputs (pulled from other tabs)

| Col | What it shows | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **A** Tier | Plan name | — |
| **B** Monthly Revenue | What user pays us per month | Tab 1 (locked prices) |
| **C** GB Consumed | How much data they actually use | Tab 2 (per-tier rates) |
| **D** Wholesale $/GB | What we pay wholesale supplier per GB | Tab 1 (blended cost) |

## Group 2 — eSIM math (subscription side)

| Col | What it shows | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **E** eSIM COGS | What we owe wholesale supplier per user | `C × D` |
| **F** Refuel Attach % | % of users who buy a 1 GB top-up | Per-tier estimate |
| **G** Refuel Revenue | Extra revenue from refuels | `F × $4.99` |
| **H** Refuel COGS | What we pay for those refuels | `F × 1 GB × D` |

## Group 3 — Profit math (the answers)

| Col | What it shows | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **I** Total Revenue | All money in | `B + G` |
| **J** Total COGS | All wholesale supplier costs | `E + H` |
| **K** Gross Profit | Profit before operating costs | `I − J` |
| **L** Gross Margin % | Profit ratio | `K / I` |
| **M** Net Margin (after OPEX) | Profit ratio after subtracting $1.80/user OPEX | `(K − 1.80) / I` |

# In plain English

| Group | Tells us |
|---|---|
| Inputs | What we charge, what they use, what it costs us |
| eSIM math | Revenue + cost for the subscription and any refuels |
| Profit math | What's left for us per user per month |

So payment processing IS in there — but **hidden inside the $1.80 OPEX blob in Column M**. You only see the impact on net margin, not the line item.

# What's MISSING from Tab 3 (you spotted it)

| Cost | Status | Why missing |
|---|---|---|
| **Marketing / acquisition** | ❌ Not in Tab 3 | Lives in "Fixed Monthly Burn" section we flagged but haven't built yet |
| **Refund cost** | ❌ Was in old Tab 3, I dropped it in rebuild | Oversight — should add back |
| **Customer acquisition cost (CAC)** | ❌ Not in Tab 3 | Lives in Tab 8 (LTV-CAC) |
| **Founder salaries / legal / insurance** | ❌ Not in Tab 3 | Fixed monthly burn, not per-user |
| **App Store fees > $1.20** | ⚠ Possibly under-modeled | If iOS is heavy, real payment cost could be $1.50+ |
| **Currency conversion / FX fees** | ❌ Not modeled | Stripe handles, but adds ~1% |
| **Tax (VAT, sales tax in some markets)** | ❌ Not modeled | Depends on geography |

# How costs SHOULD be organized in a P&L model

```
Per-user variable costs (in Tab 3 today):
  ✓ Supplier (wholesale supplier) — eSIM COGS
  ✓ Refuel supplier cost
  ✓ Payment processing (in OPEX)
  ✓ Customer support (in OPEX)
  ✓ Cloud/SMS/hosting (in OPEX)
  ✗ Refund costs ← should add
  ✗ FX fees ← minor, can skip
  ✗ Tax ← post-launch concern

Fixed monthly burn (separate section — not built):
  ✗ Founder salaries
  ✗ Legal / accounting
  ✗ Insurance
  ✗ Office / software stack
  ✗ Marketing campaigns
```

# My recommendation

Three fixes:

| Fix | What it does |
|---|---|
| **A — Split OPEX into separate columns** in Tab 3 (Payment Fees, Support, Other) | Makes the costs visible, not blob |
| **B — Add Refund Cost column back** | ~2-3% of revenue lost to refunds |
| **C — Build a "Fixed Monthly Burn" section** in Tab 1 Section 5 | Captures marketing, salaries, legal, etc. — separate from per-user OPEX |

|---|---|

- **Tab 8 (LTV-CAC)** — how long users stay
- **Tab 5 (Volume Scenarios)** — what total revenue looks like at different scales

## Turn 2031 — Assistant

Costs & Burn tab building in background. Now the explanation.

# "Tab 3 answers PER user, not how many users" — explained

## The coffee shop analogy

Imagine you open a coffee shop.

| Question | What it answers |
|---|---|
| "How much profit per cup of coffee?" | Per-unit economics. $5 sale price − $1 cost = **$4 per cup**. |
| "How many cups do we sell per day?" | Volume. 200 cups/day. |
| "What's our daily profit?" | Per-unit × volume. $4 × 200 = $800/day. |

**Tab 3 is the FIRST question.** It tells us the per-cup math.

The "200 cups per day" question (how many users) lives in **Tab 5** (Volume Scenarios) and **Tab 6** (24-Month Rollout).

## In Peanut terms

| Tab | Answers |
|---|---|
| **Tab 3 — Per-Tier Unit Economics** | "ONE Business subscriber = $21.89 profit/month" |
| **Tab 5 — Volume Scenarios** | "At 10,000 users with our mix, total profit = $X" |
| **Tab 6 — 24-Month Rollout** | "Month 1 we have 200 users, Month 12 we have 10,000, here's the running P&L" |

## Why this separation matters

You can change Tab 3 (e.g., raise Business to $34.99) and the per-user math updates → Tab 5 and Tab 6 then recalculate the total automatically.

If Tab 3 had user counts baked in, you'd be changing two things at once and couldn't isolate the impact of pricing vs growth.

# Bite 3 — Actual per-tier profit numbers

⚠ **Before showing — I have an important finding to flag.** Tab 3 now pulls the **$2.13/GB** blended wholesale from the README (based on real wholesale supplier data + 60/25/15 country mix). The Logic tabs were built earlier with $1.20/GB. These give very different results.

## Per-tier numbers at CURRENT $2.13/GB (60/25/15 mix)

| Tier | Revenue | GB Consumed | COGS @ $2.13/GB | Gross Profit | Gross Margin |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Economy (PAYG) | $4.99 | 1 | $2.13 | $2.86 | **57%** |
| Roster Bundle | $19.95 | 5 SIMs | $10.65 | $9.30 | **47%** |
| Premium Economy | $17.99 | 4 GB | $8.52 | $9.47 | **53%** |
| **Business** ⭐ | $29.99 | 6.75 GB | $14.38 | $15.61 | **52%** |
| **First** | $39.99 | 13.5 GB | $28.76 | $11.23 | **28%** ⚠ |
| **First Suite** | $49.99 | 13.2 GB | $28.12 | $21.87 | **44%** |
| **Cockpit** | $24.92 | 7.2 GB | $15.34 | $9.58 | **38%** |

## Per-tier numbers at REALISTIC Emirates mix (75/20/5 = $1.34/GB blended)

| Tier | Revenue | COGS @ $1.34/GB | Gross Profit | Gross Margin |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Economy (PAYG) | $4.99 | $1.34 | $3.65 | **73%** |
| Roster Bundle | $19.95 | $6.70 | $13.25 | **66%** |
| Premium Economy | $17.99 | $5.36 | $12.63 | **70%** |
| **Business** ⭐ | $29.99 | $9.05 | $20.94 | **70%** |
| **First** | $39.99 | $18.09 | $21.90 | **55%** |
| **First Suite** | $49.99 | $17.69 | $32.30 | **65%** |
| **Cockpit** | $24.92 | $9.65 | $15.27 | **61%** |

# The big finding

**The geo mix assumption changes everything.**

| Scenario | First tier margin |
|---|---|
| Conservative 60/25/15 mix | **28% margin** (uncomfortable) |
| Realistic 75/20/5 mix | **55% margin** (healthy) |

That's a **27 percentage point swing** based on a single placeholder assumption.

# What this means for our business

| Issue | Impact |
|---|---|
| If 15% of crew really do fly Tier-C destinations (India/Brazil/Africa) every month, First is borderline unprofitable. | Critical — we'd need to raise First price or restrict Tier-C |
| If only 5% of crew touch Tier-C destinations (more likely for Emirates), First is fine. | Akito's crew roster sampling is now URGENT — it directly determines whether First is profitable |

# Two actions worth taking right now

| # | Action | Owner | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Akito starts crew roster sampling THIS WEEK** | Akito | Direct answer to "is First profitable?" |
| 2 | **Update README mix from 60/25/15 → 75/20/5** as the new base case | Claude | More realistic for Emirates; can revert if Akito's data shows otherwise |

## Turn 2036 — Assistant

# Action #2 explained — why update from 60/25/15 to 75/20/5

## What these numbers actually mean

The percentages are **what fraction of crew trips happen in each cost zone**:

| Zone | Cost/GB | Countries |
|---|---|---|
| **Tier-A (cheap)** | $0.85 | Japan, UK, Singapore, Malaysia, Hong Kong, Korea, USA |
| **Tier-B (medium)** | $1.40 | Canada, Australia, NZ, Vietnam, UAE |
| **Tier-C (expensive)** | **$8.50** | India, Brazil, South Africa, Africa generally |

Think of it like grocery shopping:
- Imagine groceries cost $1, $1.50, or $8.50 depending on which store
- The MIX % is how often you shop at each store
- Your *blended cost* depends entirely on that mix

## The two scenarios

### CURRENT placeholder: 60% / 25% / 15%

This is the "general travel" assumption — like an average vacationer flying globally.
- 60 trips out of 100 in Tier-A
- 25 in Tier-B
- **15 in Tier-C ← here's the problem**

15% of trips in Tier-C at $8.50/GB pulls the blended average up to **$2.13/GB**.

### Proposed: 75% / 20% / 5%

This is the "Emirates crew realistic" assumption.
- 75 trips out of 100 in Tier-A (Asia + Europe + USA dominant)
- 20 in Tier-B
- **5 in Tier-C** (fewer Africa/India layovers)

Result: blended cost drops to **$1.34/GB**.

## Why 75/20/5 is more realistic for Emirates specifically

| Reason | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Emirates flies heavily to Asia/Europe/USA** | Tokyo, London, Sydney, Singapore, Hong Kong are huge Emirates routes |
| **Limited African routes** | Yes Emirates flies to 22 African countries, but with LOW frequency. Most crew never overnight in Africa. |
| **Emirates published network reality** | Roughly 70% capacity is Europe/USA/Asia, 10% Africa |
| **Crew layover concentration** | Crew overnight at major hub cities, not every served destination |

## The impact on the model — dramatic

| Metric | 60/25/15 (current) | 75/20/5 (proposed) | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blended cost/GB | $2.13 | $1.34 | **−37%** |
| First gross margin | 28% | 55% | **+27pts** |
| Business gross margin | 52% | 70% | **+18pts** |
| Premium gross margin | 53% | 70% | **+17pts** |

Every tier becomes meaningfully more profitable.

## The risk if I'm wrong

| If reality is... | Then... |
|---|---|
| Closer to 75/20/5 (likely) | Model is honest, First/Suite become attractive |
| Closer to 60/25/15 (unlikely for crew) | Model overstates margins by 15-25pts |
| Closer to 85/12/3 (very Asia-heavy crew) | Model is slightly conservative |

The danger of staying at 60/25/15: we'd UNDERESTIMATE the business and potentially make wrong pricing decisions (like raising First above $39.99 because "margins are bad").

## What "lock in 75/20/5" actually does

1. README C25, C26, C27 change to 0.75, 0.20, 0.05 (the cost values stay the same — those are real wholesale supplier numbers)
2. Blended cost (README C31) auto-recalculates from $2.13 → $1.34
3. Every Logic tab using the per-tier margins now reads ~$1.34/GB
4. Tab 3 margins improve significantly
5. Downstream tabs (Volume Scenarios, Carryover Liability) all recalculate

## Caveat (the honest part)

**Both 60/25/15 AND 75/20/5 are educated guesses.** Akito's crew roster sampling will give us the REAL number — could be 70/25/5, 80/15/5, or anything else. We're picking 75/20/5 as a more defensible base case for Emirates while we wait for real data.

| Tier | Revenue (B) | GB Consumed (C) | Wholesale (D) | eSIM COGS (E) | Refuel Net | Gross Profit (K) | **Gross Margin (L)** | Net Margin after OPEX (M) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Economy (PAYG) | $4.99 | 1 GB | $1.34 | $1.34 | — | $3.65 | **73%** | 36% |
| Roster Bundle | $19.95 | 5 SIMs | $1.34 | $6.70 | $0.37 | $13.62 | **67%** | 58% |
| **Premium Economy** | $17.99 | 4 GB | $1.34 | $5.36 | $0.73 | $13.36 | **71%** | 61% |
| **Business** ⭐ | $29.99 | 6.75 GB | $1.34 | $9.05 | $0.91 | $21.85 | **71%** | 65% |
| **First** | $39.99 | 13.5 GB | $1.34 | $18.09 | $1.46 | $23.36 | **56%** | 52% |
| **First Suite** | $49.99 | 13.2 GB | $1.34 | $17.69 | $0.73 | $33.03 | **65%** | 62% |
| **Cockpit** | $24.92 | 7.2 GB | $1.34 | $9.65 | $0.55 | $15.82 | **62%** | 55% |

## What this tells us

| Insight | Implication |
|---|---|
| **Premium / Business / Suite all at ~70% gross margin** | These are healthy SaaS-grade margins. Sustainable. |
| **Economy at 73% per top-up** | PAYG is wildly profitable per unit. Volume is the question. |
| **First at 56%** | Lowest margin tier — heavy data users eat into profit. Worth tracking. |
| **First Suite at 65% beats First at 56%** | Confirms the anchor design: First Suite is more profitable because pool dilutes per-GB consumption % |
| **Net margins (after OPEX) still healthy** | Even subtracting $1.80/user OPEX, all tiers remain 36-65% net margin |

## The "winner" tiers ranked by profit per user

| Rank | Tier | Profit/user/mo |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **First Suite** | **$33.03** ⭐ |
| 2 | **First** | $23.36 |
| 3 | **Business** | $21.85 |
| 4 | Cockpit | $15.82 |
| 5 | Roster Bundle | $13.62 |
| 6 | Premium Economy | $13.36 |
| 7 | Economy PAYG | $3.65/top-up |

**First Suite is the most profitable per-user tier** by a meaningful margin. Confirms it should be the anchor product.

## The "blended" per-user profit across all tiers

Using cabin mix (50% Economy / 18% Roster / 13% Premium / 8% Business / 3% First / 2% Suite / 3% Cockpit):

| Calc | Value |
|---|---|
| Blended profit per user/mo (weighted average) | **~$10.50** |
| Blended ARPU per user/mo | **~$17.52** |
| Blended margin | **60%** |

So on average, every paying user generates ~$10.50 gross profit per month.

## Turn 2040 — Assistant

# Bite 4 — Strategic insights & what to do with this data

The numbers are telling us 5 important things about how to run the business:

## 1. First Suite is your hero product

| | Profit per user/mo |
|---|---|
| First Suite | **$33.03** ← highest |
| First | $23.36 |
| Business | $21.85 |

**Insight:** A user upgrading from Business → First Suite generates **+$11.18 extra profit per month**. At scale, getting just 1,000 Business users to upgrade = **+$134K/year**.

**Action:** Make First Suite **prominent** on the pricing page. Use "best per-GB ($1.25)" as the headline.

## 2. Business is your default — confirm "RECOMMENDED"

Premium ($13.36) and Business ($21.85) have nearly identical margins (71%). But Business generates **$8.49 more profit per user**.

| Tier comparison | Margin | Profit/user |
|---|---|---|
| Premium Economy | 71% | $13.36 |
| **Business** | **71%** | **$21.85** ← same margin, much more $ |

**Insight:** "RECOMMENDED" badge on Business is justified. Make this tier visually the obvious choice.

## 3. First (without Suite) is your weakest tier — be cautious

| | Profit/user | Margin |
|---|---|---|
| First | $23.36 | **56%** ← lowest |
| First Suite | $33.03 | 65% |

First users consume the most absolute GB (13.5 GB), pushing COGS up. The 56% margin is fine but **lowest in the ladder**.

**Insight:** Don't market First too aggressively — push customers to Suite instead. First exists to fill the gap, not be a primary product.

## 4. Economy PAYG is a volume game

| | Per transaction | Per user/mo (2.5 top-ups) |
|---|---|---|
| Economy | $3.65 profit | ~$9.13 |

A PAYG user only makes us money if they buy multiple times. The strategic play:
- Use Economy as a **trial** funnel
- Push them to subscription within 60 days
- 50% mix on Economy means we need 5,000 PAYG users to match 500 Business users

## 5. Cockpit is fine but not the star

Cockpit profit/user = $15.82. Lower than Business ($21.85).

**Insight:** Cockpit isn't trying to maximize per-user profit — it's about **annual cash flow** ($299 upfront) and reducing churn. Different metric.

# Strategic recommendations from Tab 3 data

| Priority | Action | Owner |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Marketing site** — make First Suite the visual hero, Business the "recommended" default | Akito |
| 2 | **Pricing page UX** — show per-GB price prominently (highlights Suite's $1.25) | Akito |
| 3 | **Email funnel** — push Economy users to subscribe at month 2 | Akito + Claude |
| 4 | **Don't market First aggressively** — let users naturally land there only if they're heavy users | Akito |
| 5 | **Validate the per-tier consumption rates** post-launch via app analytics | Balraj |

# Blended business math at scale (100K users)

| Line | Value |
|---|---|
| Blended profit per user/mo | ~$10.50 |
| Annual run rate at 100K users | **$12.6M gross profit/year** |
| After per-user OPEX ($1.80) | **$10.4M net/year** |
| Minus mature fixed burn (~$320K/yr) | **~$10M net/year at 100K users** |

That's the prize.

## Turn 2045 — Assistant

# Bite 5 — How Tab 3 feeds the rest of the model

Tab 3 produces **per-user profit numbers**. These get multiplied across downstream tabs to answer different scale questions.

## The data flow

```
Tab 3 (per-user economics)  ──┐
                              ├──→  Tab 5 (Volume Scenarios)    "What if we hit 10K users?"
                              ├──→  Tab 6 (24-Month Rollout)    "Month-by-month P&L"
                              └──→  Tab 8 (LTV-CAC + Churn)     "Lifetime value per tier"
```

## Concrete example — Business tier ($21.85 profit/user/mo)

Imagine you have 10,000 paying users. With our locked mix:

| Step | Calc | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **Tab 3** says | Business profit/user/mo | $21.85 |
| **Tab 1** mix | Business = 8% of users | 800 Business users |
| **Tab 5** multiplies | 800 × $21.85 | **$17,480/month from Business tier alone** |
| **Tab 6** spreads over time | Month 1 = 200 users, ... Month 12 = 10,000 users | Monthly P&L curve |
| **Tab 8** × lifetime | If Business users stay 18 months avg | **LTV = $393/user** |

## What each downstream tab does specifically

| Downstream tab | Reads from Tab 3 | Produces |
|---|---|---|
| **Tab 5 — Volume Scenarios** | Profit per tier × user counts (1K/5K/10K/100K) | Total revenue & profit at each scale |
| **Tab 6 — 24-Month Rollout** | Per-tier profit × monthly user growth curve | Cumulative cash + profit over 2 years |
| **Tab 8 — LTV-CAC + Churn** | Per-tier profit × tier lifetime | LTV per tier (used vs CAC for payback) |
| **Tab 10 — Carryover Liability** | GB Carries from Tab 2 + Tab 3 profit | Balance sheet liability over time |
| **Tab 11 — Strategic Scenarios** | Compares Tab 3 under different supplier paths | wholesale supplier-vs-direct comparison |

## Why this layering matters

If we want to ask **"what happens if we get 50,000 users?"** — we don't rebuild the model. We change a single user-count input in Tab 5, and:

- Tab 5 multiplies Tab 3 numbers by 50K
- Tab 6 redraws the growth curve
- Tab 8 updates LTV
- Cash flow projections in Tab 12 reflate

**Tab 3 is the foundation. The rest is just scaling math.**

## Concrete profit picture at 100K users (using cabin mix)

| Tier | Users (% of 100K) | Profit/user/mo | Tier monthly profit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Economy | 50,000 (50%) | $3.65 × 2.5 = $9.13 | $456,500 |
| Roster Bundle | 18,000 | $13.62 | $245,160 |
| Premium Economy | 13,000 | $13.36 | $173,680 |
| Business ⭐ | 8,000 | $21.85 | $174,800 |
| First | 3,000 | $23.36 | $70,080 |
| First Suite | 2,000 | $33.03 | $66,060 |
| Cockpit | 3,000 | $15.82 | $47,460 |
| **TOTAL gross profit** | 100,000 | | **~$1.23M/month** |
| **TOTAL annual gross profit** | | | **~$14.8M/year** |

After OPEX ($1.80 × 100K = $180K/mo) and fixed burn (~$26K/mo mature):
**Net profit ≈ $1.02M/month = ~$12.3M/year at 100K users**

# That's the business case in one table.

| Cockpit | $25.67 | $15.80 | 62% |

## Bite 4 — What it tells us

> **First Suite = highest profit per user ($33). Business = default sweet spot (70% margin). First = weakest. Push customers UP the ladder.**

## Bite 5 — Where it goes

> Tab 3 produces per-user numbers. **Tab 5** multiplies by user counts → total revenue. **Tab 8** multiplies by lifetime → LTV.

# Bite 6 — Sensitivity Analysis

> What happens to per-tier profit if key assumptions move?

## Scenario 1 — Consumption % rises (users eat more)

If Premium Economy consumption goes **40% → 50%**:

| | Now | If 50% | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| GB Consumed | 4 GB | 5 GB | +1 GB |
| Wholesale COGS | $5.36 | $6.70 | +$1.34 |
| Gross Profit | $13.35 | $12.01 | **-$1.34/user** |
| At 100K users × 13% Premium | | | **-$20,904/year** |

## Scenario 2 — Refuel attach drops (users top up less)

If Business refuel attach goes **25% → 10%**:

| | Now | If 10% | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Refuel Revenue | $1.25 | $0.50 | -$0.75 |
| Refuel net profit | $0.91 | $0.37 | **-$0.54/user** |
| At 100K users × 8% Business | | | **-$5,184/year** |

## Scenario 3 — Wholesale rises (supplier hikes prices)

If wholesale supplier raises blended cost **$1.34 → $2.00** (50% hike):

| Tier | Profit now | Profit at $2.00 | Δ margin |
|---|---|---|---|
| Premium | $13.35 (70%) | $10.95 (58%) | -12 pts |
| Business | $21.84 (70%) | $17.39 (56%) | -14 pts |
| First | $23.33 (56%) | $14.43 (34%) | **-22 pts** ⚠ |
| First Suite | $33.00 (65%) | $24.29 (48%) | -17 pts |

**Insight:** First gets crushed if wholesale rises (already lowest margin). Argues for moving direct to eSIM Access at scale.

## Scenario 4 — Mix shift toward heavy tiers

If First grows from **3% → 6%** of users (heavy crew adoption):

| | At 3% First | At 6% First | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| First tier annual contribution at 100K users | $70,080 | $140,160 | **+$70,080/yr** |

But First also has lowest margin. Net positive on revenue, but check that margins hold.

## Scenario 5 — Price move on Premium Economy

If we drop Premium from **$17.99 → $14.99** (to drive volume):

| | $17.99 | $14.99 | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross profit/user | $13.35 | $10.35 | -$3.00 |
| Need volume increase of | — | **+29%** | to break even |

**Insight:** Premium is fragile to price drops. Don't promo Premium — promo Business or First.

## The 5 sensitivity tests in summary

| Risk | What we'd do if it materializes |
|---|---|
| Consumption rises | Accept thinner margins (or pull friction levers harder) |
| Refuel drops | Marketing push for top-ups + raise refuel price |
| Wholesale rises | Accelerate move to direct (eSIM Access) supplier |
| Mix shifts heavy | Push First Suite over First (better margin on heavy tier) |
| Price war | Don't drop Premium — drop Roster Bundle instead |

# Bite 6 = "what could break us, and how to respond." Ready to wait for the blueprint agent to finish + I'll patch this in, or move on?

| # | Tab | What it does |

| 6 | **Roster Bundle Logic** | $3.99/flight — 5-flight worked example + commitment vs PAYG |
| 7 | **Premium Economy Logic** | $17.99/10GB — 1-GB refill UX + transparency policy |
| 8 | **Business Logic** | $29.99/15GB — RECOMMENDED tier rationale |
| 9 | **First Logic** | $39.99/25GB — concierge layer + 2-tier First strategy |
| 10 | **First Suite Logic** | $49.99/40GB — anchor product with best per-GB ($1.25) |
| 11 | **Cockpit Logic** | $299/yr annual lockin + 12GB emergency bank (refreshes annually) |

| 21 | **Strategic Scenarios** | wholesale supplier-only vs hybrid vs direct supplier comparison |

# Volume Scenarios

## Bite 1 — What this tab does

In one line:

> **Total business math.** Take Tab 3's per-USER profit and multiply by user counts to see "what does the whole business look like at 100 users? 1,000? 5,000? 20,000?"

Tab 3 = profit per ONE crew member.
**Volume Scenarios = profit × how many crew members.**

# How it's structured

The tab has **4 user-count scenarios** stacked as rows:

| Scenario | # of paying users |
|---|---|
| **Conservative** | 100 |
| **Base** | 1,000 |
| **Aggressive** | 5,000 |
| **Hockey Stick** | 20,000 |

For each row, it computes:

| Column | What it shows |
|---|---|
| B | Active Users |
| C | Monthly Revenue (= users × blended ARPU) |
| D | Monthly COGS (= users × blended COGS) |
| E | Monthly Gross Profit |
| F | Gross Margin % |
| G | Monthly OPEX (= users × $1.80) |
| H | Monthly Net Profit |
| I | Net Margin % |
| J | **Annual Net Profit** ← the headline number |

In Tab 3, Economy shows monthly revenue at **$4.99** (one transaction). But the realistic Economy USER buys **2.5 top-ups per month** = ~$12.48/month.

If we correct for this:

| Per user/month | Currently | Corrected |
|---|---|---|
| Blended Revenue | $14.19 | **$17.94** |
| Blended Net Profit | $7.79 | **~$10.50** |

That would push **Annual Net at 100K from $9.35M → $12.6M.**

| Business | 8% | 80 |
| First | 3% | 30 |
| First Suite | 2% | 20 |
| Cockpit | 3% | 30 |
| **Total** | 100% | **1,000** |

| Tier | Profit per user | Total this tier |
|---|---|---|
| Economy | $3.65 | 500 × $3.65 = **$1,825** |
| Roster Bundle | $13.60 | 180 × $13.60 = **$2,448** |
| Premium Economy | $13.35 | 130 × $13.35 = **$1,736** |
| Business | $21.84 | 80 × $21.84 = **$1,747** |
| First | $23.33 | 30 × $23.33 = **$700** |
| First Suite | $33.00 | 20 × $33.00 = **$660** |
| Cockpit | $15.80 | 30 × $15.80 = **$474** |
| **TOTAL Monthly Gross Profit** | | **~$9,590** |

|---|---|
| Gross profit | $9,590 |
| OPEX (1,000 × $1.80) | -$1,800 |
| **Net profit / month** | **$7,790** |
| **Net profit / year** | **$93,480** |

## Turn 2100 — User

Fix Economy under-modeling ($4.99 → $12.48 per user/mo to reflect 2.5 top-ups) explain this with example

## Turn 2101 — Assistant

# The Economy under-modeling problem — explained with example

## What's happening in Tab 3 right now

Look at the Economy row:

| Field | Value | What it represents |
|---|---|---|
| Monthly Revenue | **$4.99** | This is ONE top-up's worth |
| GB Consumed | 1.0 GB | One top-up = 1 GB |
| Profit | $3.65 | Profit on ONE top-up |

**The model is treating Economy like the user buys exactly 1 GB per month.** That's wrong — PAYG users buy multiple top-ups per month as they fly.

## Real Economy user behavior

A typical crew member on Economy (PAYG) has 8-12 layovers/month. They buy data per trip. Average:

| Crew type | Top-ups per month |
|---|---|
| Light Economy (cautious) | 1-2 |
| Average Economy | 2-3 |
| Heavy Economy (lots of layovers) | 4-5 |
| **Blended average** | **~2.5 top-ups/month** |

So a real Economy user generates **~2.5 × $4.99 = $12.48 in monthly revenue**, not $4.99.

## Concrete example — 100 Economy users in a month

### Current model (single transaction):

| Item | Calc | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue per user | $4.99 | $4.99 |
| 100 users | 100 × $4.99 | **$499/month** |
| Annual | × 12 | **$5,988/year** |

### Reality (2.5 top-ups average):

| Item | Calc | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue per user | 2.5 × $4.99 | $12.48 |
| 100 users | 100 × $12.48 | **$1,248/month** |
| Annual | × 12 | **$14,976/year** |

**The model is UNDER-counting by $749/month = $8,988/year per 100 Economy users.**

## At scale — this is HUGE

Economy = 50% of users. So at:

| Total users | Economy users | Annual revenue (current) | Annual revenue (corrected) | **Annual loss in model** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10,000 | 5,000 | $299K | $749K | **−$450K** ⚠ |
| 50,000 | 25,000 | $1.5M | $3.7M | **−$2.2M** ⚠ |
| **100,000** | **50,000** | **$2.99M** | **$7.49M** | **−$4.5M** ⚠ |

**The model is under-counting Economy revenue by $4.5M/year at 100K users.**

## Why this happened

When I built Tab 3, I modeled each tier as "1 user, 1 transaction per month." That works for subscriptions (Premium, Business, etc. — they're each ONE payment per month). But Economy is PAYG — multiple transactions per user per month. I forgot to multiply by transaction frequency for the PAYG line.

## Two fix paths

| Option | What it does |
|---|---|
| **A** — Hardcode Economy at $12.48/month in Tab 3 | Quick fix, but loses the per-transaction detail |
| **B** — Add a "Transactions per User per Month" column to Tab 3, set to 2.5 for Economy and 1 for subscriptions | Cleaner — preserves transaction structure |

## Turn 2112 — Assistant

# Tab 6 — 24-Month Rollout

## Bite 1 — What this tab does

> **Time-based P&L.** Tab 5 (Volume Scenarios) showed snapshots at different sizes. Tab 6 shows the **monthly journey** from launch to month 24.

Each row = 1 month. For 24 months we track:

| Column | What it shows |
|---|---|
| A | Month (M1-M24) |
| B | Active users that month |
| C | Monthly Revenue |
| D | Monthly COGS |
| E | Monthly OPEX |
| F | Monthly Net Profit |
| G | **Cumulative Profit (running total)** |

The cumulative column is the most important — it tells us **when we turn the corner from loss to profit**.

# The growth assumption (currently in the sheet)

| Phase | Months | Users at end |
|---|---|---|
| **Launch ramp** | M1-M6 | 50 → 450 |
| **Word-of-mouth growth** | M7-M12 | 650 → 2,100 |
| **Multi-airline expansion** | M13-M18 | 2,400 → 3,900 |
| **Approaching saturation** | M19-M24 | 4,200 → 5,000 |

S-curve ending at **5,000 users at month 24**.

# Ready for Bite 2 — the actual monthly numbers and when we hit breakeven?

## Turn 2114 — Assistant

# Bite 2 — The 24-month journey with example numbers

Let me walk through 4 key months as a story:

## 📅 Month 1 — Launch day

| Line | Math | $ |
|---|---|---|
| Active users | (Akito + first 50 crew from WhatsApp) | **50** |
| Monthly Revenue | 50 × ~$17.94 blended | $897 |
| Monthly COGS (supplier) | 50 × ~$5.61 blended | $281 |
| Monthly OPEX | 50 × $1.80 | $90 |
| **Net Profit** | $897 − $281 − $90 | **$526** ✅ |
| Cumulative | | **$526** |

**Even in month 1 with 50 users, we make $526 net profit** (per the model — ignoring founder salaries which aren't in this tab).

## 📅 Month 6 — Word getting around

| Line | Math | $ |
|---|---|---|
| Active users | Growth from Emirates network referrals | **450** |
| Monthly Revenue | 450 × $17.94 | $8,073 |
| Monthly COGS | 450 × $5.61 | $2,525 |
| Monthly OPEX | 450 × $1.80 | $810 |
| **Net Profit** | | **$4,738** |
| Cumulative (M1-M6 sum) | | **~$13,000** |

Six months in: ~$13K total in the bank. The business is real.

## 📅 Month 12 — End of Year 1

| Line | Math | $ |
|---|---|---|
| Active users | Multi-airline expansion kicking in | **2,100** |
| Monthly Revenue | 2,100 × $17.94 | $37,674 |
| Monthly COGS | 2,100 × $5.61 | $11,781 |
| Monthly OPEX | 2,100 × $1.80 | $3,780 |
| **Net Profit** | | **$22,113/mo** |
| Cumulative (M1-M12) | | **~$95K** |

End of Year 1: making **$22K/month**, cumulative **~$95K** in the bank.

## 📅 Month 24 — End of Year 2

| Line | Math | $ |
|---|---|---|
| Active users | Approaching saturation in our crew vertical | **5,000** |
| Monthly Revenue | 5,000 × $17.94 | $89,700 |
| Monthly COGS | 5,000 × $5.61 | $28,050 |
| Monthly OPEX | 5,000 × $1.80 | $9,000 |
| **Net Profit** | | **$52,650/mo** |
| Cumulative (M1-M24) | | **~$700K-900K** |

End of Year 2: **$52K/month run rate**, **~$800K total cumulative profit** over the full 24 months.

# But wait — there's a missing cost

Tab 6 doesn't include **Fixed Monthly Burn** (founder salaries, legal, insurance — from Costs & Burn tab).

If we add early-stage burn of **$11K/month**:

| Month | Revenue Profit | Minus Fixed Burn | Real Net |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 (50 users) | $526 | -$11,000 | **-$10,474** 🔴 |
| M6 (450 users) | $4,738 | -$11,000 | **-$6,262** 🔴 |
| **M11-12 (~2,000 users)** | **$22,000** | **-$11,000** | **+$11,000** 🟢 ← **TRUE BREAKEVEN** |
| M24 (5,000 users) | $52,650 | -$11,000 | $41,650 🟢 |

**Real breakeven hits around Month 11-12** when we have ~2,000 paying users.

# Want me to add fixed burn into Tab 6 to make it more honest, or move on to Bite 3 — when we hit specific milestones?

```
Now that all 29 CrewLink screens are generated, run a cross-screen quality pass in this order. After each pass, fix the violations you find without asking — keep going.

1. ILLUSTRATION CONSISTENCY
Every hero illustration must share the same painterly watercolor language — soft brush strokes, cream backgrounds, organic blob crops (never plain rectangles), red + gold + navy accents only. Flag any screen where the hero used a flat tech illustration, photo, gradient mesh, or generic stock-style art, and regenerate that hero in the locked watercolor style. Special attention: Screen 11 (Tokyo Tower), Screen 9 (flag watercolors), Screen 22 (hotel exteriors), Screen 24 (rec thumbnails) — these are the most likely to drift.

2. TYPOGRAPHY HIERARCHY
Verify across all screens:
- Headlines: Manrope 700, 24–28pt mobile
- Body: Inter 400, 14–16pt
- Caps labels: JetBrains Mono 11pt, letter-spacing 0.08em, gold #C9A03B (except inside red-filled cards where they go cream/white)
- Numerics (prices, GB amounts): Manrope 700, 28–48pt, letter-spacing -0.02em
If any screen drifts (default sans, missing mono treatment on labels, wrong size), fix it.

3. COLOR DISCIPLINE
- Red #D71921: only on primary CTAs and the Roster Bundle full-red card (Screen 6 card 2). NOT as a large background anywhere else except Splash (Screen 1).
- Gold #C9A03B: only on RECOMMENDED ribbons, prestige tier accents, mono caps labels, and gold-trim circles.
- Navy #2A2A4A: only on Business tier accents and technical chrome (signal bars, secondary text).
- Burgundy #7A1F2B: only on First tier pill.
- Indigo + Pilot-gold #D4AF37: only on Cockpit tier.
Flag any screen using these colors outside their scope and recolor.

4. MICROCOPY VOICE
Every body copy and button label should sound like a Senior Cabin Crew friend — warm, "you" framing not "we", aviation idioms where natural (welcome aboard, fly your own plan, refuel, all aboard). Remove any generic SaaS phrasing ("Get started", "Learn more", "Welcome to CrewLink!", "Click here") and rewrite in crew-insider tone. Never use the word "Emirates" in copy — say "your airline".

5. BOTTOM NAV CONSISTENCY
- Bottom nav present on: Home (5), Plans (6), eSIM Library (14), Hotels list (22), Crew Recs (24)
- Bottom nav HIDDEN on: Splash (1), Signup (2), Email Sent (3), Verify+OTP (4), all flow screens, all modals, all bottom sheets, success states, AI chat, refund flows
Verify and fix.

6. ACCESSIBILITY
- All touch targets ≥ 44px
- Body text ≥ 14pt
- Contrast meets WCAG AA (red CTAs on cream, gold on cream — check)
- Bottom nav icons have labels (Home / Plans / eSIMs / Hotels / Profile)

7. STATE & STATUS COHERENCE
- Status pills: ACTIVE soft green tint, DORMANT soft gray, EXPIRED soft red — all caps mono
- All cards have 14px corner radius (24px on hero/bottom-sheet tops)
- All buttons + status pills are fully pill-shaped (9999px)
- No sharp 0px corners anywhere

After all 7 passes, output a numbered list: which screens you changed and what you fixed on each. Then ask if I want to spot-fix any specific screen further.
```

## Turn 2197 — Assistant

## Turn 2198 — Assistant

Found the bug. When I built index.html, the `<section>` tag had `class="screen"` AND the body's class merged in — browsers take only the last `class` attribute, so the `.screen` class was getting overwritten. That's why ALL 29 screens were visible at once and clicks went anywhere. Let me rebuild it correctly.

---

## Turn 2199 — Assistant

Pushed. The actual bug: my `<section>` tag had **two** `class=` attributes — one with `.screen` and one inherited from the Stitch body — and the browser was keeping only the second one. That meant `.screen` class never applied, so all 29 screens rendered stacked on top of each other. Random clicks would fire buttons from any layer, hence "jumbled."

Now fixed:
1. **Class lists properly merged** into a single class attribute → only the active screen is visible
2. **90 explicit `data-go="<slug>"`** attributes injected into the right primary CTAs across all 29 screens (matches v3 exactly)
3. **JS simplified to v3's pattern**: only `[data-go]` elements navigate, everything else is ignored

Hard refresh once it deploys (~30-60s).

## Turn 2219 — Assistant

Two honest reads:

- **Starlink Next** is a hard no — SpaceX aggressively defends Starlink. You'd get a C&D inside a quarter. Same lane, but you can't use that exact word.
- **Moonlight** as a company name is poetic and expandable, but it's one of the most-trademarked words in the world (films, skincare, dozens of apps). Whether it's usable depends entirely on Class 9 (software) + Class 38 (telecom) being clear. Worth checking, but I wouldn't fall in love with it before verification.

The cleaner pattern is what's already in your memory: **holding co** (abstract, expandable, doesn't matter what it means) ≠ **service name** (punchy, memorable, sky/travel-themed). Apple Inc. owns iPhone. SpaceX owns Starlink. The shapes are different on purpose.

# Want me to run availability checks on a celestial shortlist?

---

## Turn 2220 — Assistant

Here are 10 fresh candidates — mix of cosmic, sky, light, and invented words. I tagged each with vibe and best fit (service vs. holding-co):

| # | Name | Vibe | Roots | Best for | Why it works |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Halo** | aspirational, orbital ring | English | Service or both | Universally understood, ring = satellite orbit metaphor, premium feel |
| 2 | **Aurora** | sky-travel, romance, premium | Latin | Service | Northern lights = travel-as-magic; warmer than Starlink |
| 3 | **Vega** | bright star, sharp, four-letter | Star name | Service or company | One of the brightest stars; short, easy to spell globally |
| 4 | **Cirrus** | cloud, light, unique angle | Latin | Service | Skipping the crowded "star/sat" lane → cloud = lighter, distinct |
| 5 | **Lumen** | light, modern tech, clean | Latin (unit of light) | Service | Strong tech-startup feel; "give light" pairs well with connectivity |
| 6 | **Sora** (空) | JP for "sky", global, soft | Japanese | Both | Akito-resonant, two syllables, no hard consonants, scales globally |
| 7 | **Yonder** | distant, exploratory, "wild blue yonder" | English | Service | Direction word, evocative — feels like a destination not a product |
| 8 | **Selene** | Greek moon goddess | Greek myth | Holding company | The "Moonlight" feel you wanted, but with stronger heritage + fewer TMs |
| 9 | **Vesper** | evening star, refined | Latin / English | Service | Bond Vesper martini connotation = sophistication; star reference |
| 10 | **Voyo** | invented, voyage-adjacent | Made-up | Both | Made-up like SORU/ROMU; short, fun, expandable to anything |

**My read:** of these, **Sora (空)** is the strongest dual-purpose pick — short, both founders relate to it, "sky" maps directly to connectivity + travel, and the JP angle differentiates you from every Western startup in this lane. **Vega** is the closest analog to "Starlink" energy without the TM landmine. **Selene** is the right move if you want to keep "Moonlight" as a feeling without the trademark fight.

Watch out: Halo (game), Aurora (HP printer + AWS service), Lumen (Lumen5), Vesper (lots of TMs) all have known conflicts. Verification will sort them. **Tell me which ones you want me to research** (pick any number) and I'll send a Researcher.

## Turn 2233 — Assistant

Two layers — different answers:

- **Legal entity**: needs the qualifier. Just "Moonlight LLC" is likely taken in Delaware and clashes with the existing "Moonlight Ventures LLC" Class 35 mark. So legally it's **Moonlight Holdings LLC**.
- **Public brand**: you can call yourselves just **"Moonlight"** everywhere — website, decks, bios, app. Apple's legal entity is "Apple Inc." but nobody says that. Same pattern.

So yes, day-to-day = **Moonlight**. On paperwork = **Moonlight Holdings LLC**.

| | Sandbox / staging environment access | Required for dev + Apple review | 🔴 |
| | Webhook events for activation, depletion, expiry | Powers your in-app status + low-data alerts | 🔴 |
| | Rate limits (requests/sec, daily caps) | Affects bulk roster bundle (5 eSIMs in one tap) | 🟡 |
| | eSIM delivery format — LPA string, QR, Universal Link, or all three? | iOS 17.4 Universal Link is the holy grail (no Carrier Entitlement needed) | 🔴 |
| **Coverage** | Country + carrier matrix per country (which MNO are they using?) | Crew SIM publishes this; you need parity to win | 🔴 |
| | 5G availability per country | Premium tier story | 🟡 |
| | Hotspot/tethering policy per country | Crew specifically need this for hotel WiFi | 🟡 |
| | Throttling rules at quota limits | Refund/recovery UX depends on this | 🟡 |
| **Lifecycle** | Top-up support — can users add data to existing eSIM, or is each purchase a new install? | Big UX difference; new install per top-up is painful | 🔴 |
| | Activation trigger — first install, first connect, or first data byte? | Drives your "Activate Japan eSIM?" warning modal | 🔴 |
| | Multi-device install / reissue if user wipes phone | Affects your "Failed eSIM Recovery" flow | 🟡 |
| **Reporting** | Per-eSIM usage data — real-time or polled? Latency? | Drives your in-app data meter | 🟡 |
| | Failed activation visibility (you see the error, or just "didn't work"?) | Required to auto-refund + reissue | 🔴 |
| **Support / SLA** | Response time guarantee for incidents | Crew users in flight = high-stakes failures | 🟡 |
| | Network outage notifications (push or just status page?) | Avoids hours of inbound tickets | 🟡 |
| **Contract / Legal** | Reseller agreement terms — exclusivity, geo, white-label rights | You want to brand as your own, no "Powered by wholesale supplier" footer | 🔴 |
| | Can you sign direct with eSIM Access instead of through them? | This is the **40% markup play** — direct = 3.5× margin per memory | 🔴 |
| | GDPR / UAE PDPL / CCPA compliance posture | Required for app store + UAE TRA | 🔴 |
| | Termination clause (notice period, data export) | Avoids lock-in trap | 🟡 |

| | Other large clients — anyone in airline/travel-crew vertical? | Conflict check before sharing strategy | 🟡 |
| | Will they sign NDA to discuss roadmap? | Lets you share crew thesis without leaking | 🟢 |

## Turn 2248 — Assistant

Here's a paste-ready pricing section. Tight, modular — each point wholesale supplier can answer separately. I kept it neutral (no tip-off about your direct-bypass thesis — save that for a different conversation).

---

**Subject: Pricing & commercials questions ahead of v1 launch**

Hi [name],

As we firm up our v1 launch plan, we'd like to lock down the unit economics. A few asks on pricing:

1. **Full rate sheet, machine-readable.** Could you share the current wholesale rate sheet as CSV or JSON — covering all supported countries, GB tiers (500MB / 1GB / 3GB / 5GB / 10GB / unlimited / regional bundles), and validity windows (1d / 3d / 7d / 14d / 30d)? Our existing PDF reference works but isn't great for modeling.

2. **Anchor SKU pricing.** Specifically the wholesale rate on **1 GB / 1-day / per-order eSIM** for the top 30 destinations we'll launch with (Japan, UK, Thailand, Mauritius, Sri Lanka, Hong Kong, Singapore, Malaysia, India, Australia, France, Germany, Italy, Spain, USA, Canada, UAE roaming, KSA, etc.). We're modeling our pay-per-trip price point first, and this SKU drives the math.

3. **Volume tier curve.** What does the discount look like at scale? If you could share illustrative pricing at **1K / 10K / 50K activations per month**, we can model the inflection points and plan our growth ramp accordingly.

4. **Currency, billing cycle, payment terms.** Billing in USD or JPY? Prepay-only or NET 30 available? Minimum monthly commitment or "use-it-or-lose-it" clauses?

5. **Refund and credit policy.** What's the wholesale-cost treatment for: (a) **failed activations**, (b) eSIMs that **expire unused**, (c) **bulk orders cancelled before activation**? We're designing a refund-first UX (Apple-style ease) so this is material to our cost model.

Happy to share our cabin-class subscription structure and forecast volume curve once we hear back — gives you context for how we'd ramp through 2026.

Thanks,
Balraj

---

- "Could we contract direct with eSIM Access?" — burns the relationship
- "What's your margin on top of eSIM Access wholesale?" — same

## Turn 2252 — Assistant

Real numbers found. **Surprise**: wholesale supplier's actual markup on Japan 1GB/1-day is **67–75%, not 40%**. Their public "40% margin" pitch doesn't match what's in the rate sheet.

Here's the picture for your anchor SKU (Japan, 1GB / 1-day):

| Layer | Cost (CNY) | Cost (USD ≈ ¥7.25) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| wholesale supplier upstream settlement | 4.0 | **$0.55** | They source via Multi-IMSI |
| wholesale supplier charges YOU | 7.0 | **$0.97** | 75% markup over their cost |
| Your retail (Economy locked) | — | **$4.99** | Per cabin-class structure |
| **Your gross margin** | — | **$4.02 (81%)** | Per activation |
| If you went direct (bypass) | — | **$0.55** | Margin → $4.44 (89%), +$0.42/unit |

The picture is actually a lot better than the "40% margin" framing suggested — at $4.99 retail your margins are healthy. **But wholesale supplier is skimming 67–75% from cost, not 40%.** That's the real leverage.

**Three levers for your negotiation:**
1. **Push markup down**: 75% → 40% saves you ~$0.30/unit on Japan. At 50K units/mo = ~$15K/mo
2. **No ultra-cheap 1-day SKU**: cheapest is 6 CNY ($0.83). You can't build a "$2 trial" Economy without one
3. **5G premium gap**: SoftBank 5G is +50% over 4G — pricing lever for Premium Economy

# Tab 1 — README & Assumptions

**What it is**: The single input layer. Every other tab pulls its numbers from here. Change anything yellow → everything downstream updates.

**Three sections you should understand:**

1. **Cabin Class Retail Prices** (rows 14–20) — your 7 locked tiers + their pool sizes + carry-over %. The carry-over column matters: Premium = 30%, Business = 50%, First = 80%, Cockpit = 100%. Higher carry = more breakage protection for users + more liability for you.

2. **Geo Cost Buckets** (rows 25–28) — Tier-A cheap ($0.97/GB), Tier-B medium ($1.50), Tier-C expensive ($5.80). The default mix is 60/25/15. The **blended cost in row 29** is the number that drives all COGS calculations.

3. **Cabin-Class Mix** (rows 34–40) — what % of your paying users are in each tier. Default assumes Roster Bundle leads (35%), Economy second (30%). If your real launch traffic is different, change these and re-look.

**Don't touch**: anything in black (formulas), anything in green (cross-sheet refs).

- At 1,000 users = $1,100/mo (still manageable)

## Turn 2278 — Assistant

Let me walk through each cost with the "why" behind the number.

## 1. Cloud / API — $0.15 per user per month

**What it is**: Your backend servers. Every time a crew member opens the app, your servers do work — check their account, fetch their eSIMs, talk to the wholesaler's API to provision a new eSIM.

**Where $0.15 comes from**: A bare-bones eSIM backend (Node.js or Python on AWS, Postgres database, Redis cache) runs about $150–200/month at 1,000 active users. Divide by users = ~$0.15. That's mostly database + API gateway charges. As you grow, this scales sub-linearly (the database doesn't double if users double) so unit cost DROPS at scale — could be $0.05 at 10K users.

## 2. Payment processing — $0.50 per user per month

**What it is**: Stripe (or whoever processes your payments) takes a cut every time money moves. Their fee is **2.9% of the transaction + $0.30 flat**.

**Where $0.50 comes from**: On an average transaction of ~$15 (mix of $5 Economy buys and $25 Business monthly subs), Stripe takes $0.30 + (2.9% × $15) = $0.74 per transaction. But not every user transacts every month — Cockpit Annual users pay once a year, some users skip months. So averaging across all users, it's around $0.50/user/month.

**Watch out**: International cards (most of your users) add an extra 1.5%. Currency conversion adds another 1%. The real number could creep to $0.70-$0.80 if you don't use Stripe Atlas + USD-only pricing.

## 3. Customer support — $0.30 per user per month

**What it is**: Someone (or some AI) has to answer "my eSIM isn't working" tickets. Failed activations, billing questions, refund requests, "how do I install this."

**Where $0.30 comes from**: Industry rule of thumb — about 5% of users contact support each month. Each ticket costs roughly $5–8 to resolve (human time or escalated AI calls). So 5% × $6 average = $0.30 per user.

**At 1K users**: 50 tickets/month — handleable by founders + an AI chatbot for the easy stuff. **At 10K users**: 500 tickets/month — you need a part-time support hire. This number stops being accurate around 5K users; you'd staff up and your "real" support cost would be salary-driven instead.

## 4. SMS / OTP / verification — $0.05 per user per month

**What it is**: When a new user signs up, you send them an SMS code to verify their phone. Same when they verify their crew email. These messages cost money — about $0.02 each via Twilio.

**Where $0.05 comes from**: Each user generates ~2-3 SMS messages over their lifecycle (signup + crew verification + occasional re-auth). Amortize over their average lifetime = $0.05/month. Tiny but always there.

## 5. Hosting + dev tools — $0.10 per user per month

**What it is**: The fixed costs of running a software business — GitHub for code, Sentry for error monitoring, Vercel for the marketing site, Apple Developer account ($99/year), your domain renewal, SSL certificates.

**Where $0.10 comes from**: These are mostly fixed — maybe $1,000-1,500/year total. Divided across users it lands at ~$0.10 at 1K users. **This number gets smaller fast as you scale** — at 10K users it's $0.01.

---

**Total = $1.10 per user per month** is the **per-user variable opex baseline**. Think of it as: every paying user costs you $1.10 to "exist" before they even consume any eSIM data.

**The bigger picture**: when you do the unit economics math (Tab 3), that $1.10 comes off the gross profit after eSIM COGS. So Business tier at $24.99 retail with ~$9 eSIM cost = $16 gross profit per user, minus $1.10 opex = $14.90 net contribution per user. That's the SaaS metric that drives your investment story.

**What's NOT included** (and you need to budget separately):
- **Founder + future salaries** — biggest hidden cost
- **Marketing / customer acquisition** — modeled in Tab 8 (LTV-CAC)
- **Office, legal, accounting, Delaware franchise tax** — corporate overhead
- **Failed-eSIM goodwill refunds** — modeled in Tab 12
